If true, users lacking rights to any required phase cannot view the page; if false, authorization is deferred until the function is invoked, so the page may display but fail on submit. Notes default TRUE, no security risk but auditing changes: deferred checks always log failures, which can add noise to AuthorisationLog. Also briefly covers ENABLE_SELECT_ALL_CHECKBOX for multiselect lists.

children.title: ENABLE_SELECT_ALL_CHECKBOX
children.summary: Describes the ENABLE_SELECT_ALL_CHECKBOX configuration for the multi-select check-box widget in Cúram. When set to true in curam-config.xml, it adds a column-header check-box to list widgets so users can select or clear all items at once. If omitted, the setting defaults to false. Refers readers to the MULTISELECT widget documentation for more details.

children.title: TRANSFER_LISTS_MODE
children.summary: Defines global UI configuration flags: TRANSFER_LISTS_MODE renders all multi-select controls as Transfer List widgets (default false), HIDE_CONDITIONAL_LINKS hides false conditional links or shows them disabled (default true), and DISABLE_AUTO_COMPLETE turns off input autocomplete (default false). Also documents SCROLLBAR_CONFIG, which enables vertical scrollbars for LIST or CLUSTER elements via up to two ENABLE_SCROLLBARS entries with TYPE and MAX_HEIGHT attributes; otherwise no scrollbar is shown. Begins PAGINATION settings for application-wide list pagination, overridable by individual lists.

children.title: HIDE_CONDITIONAL_LINKS
children.summary: Configures application UI behavior for conditional links. HIDE_CONDITIONAL_LINKS controls whether false-evaluating conditional links are hidden (true) or shown disabled (false); default is true if omitted. This section also appears alongside related UI settings like transfer lists, auto-complete, scrollbar configuration, and pagination.

children.title: DISABLE_AUTO_COMPLETE
children.summary: Configures application-wide auto-complete behavior. DISABLE_AUTO_COMPLETE disables auto complete on all input fields when true, enables it when false, and defaults to FALSE if omitted. This section appears alongside other global UI settings like transfer lists, conditional links, and scrollbar configuration, and is useful for determining whether input fields will suppress browser auto-completion.

children.title: SCROLLBAR_CONFIG
children.summary: Configures application-wide scrollbar behavior for LIST and CLUSTER elements. SCROLLBAR_CONFIG can include up to two ENABLE_SCROLLBARS entries, each specifying TYPE (LIST or CLUSTER) and MAX_HEIGHT to trigger a vertical scrollbar once the element exceeds that height. Example shows separate limits for LIST and CLUSTER. If omitted, no LIST or CLUSTER displays a vertical scrollbar.

children.title: PAGINATION
children.summary: This section begins the global pagination configuration for LIST components in the application. It states that pagination settings apply system-wide, but individual lists can override the global defaults. The surrounding page also covers related UI behavior settings such as transfer lists, conditional link visibility, auto-complete disabling, and scrollbar configuration for LIST/CLUSTER elements.

General configuration covers web client config files and curam-config.xml settings. It explains where to place custom application artifacts, how clientapps overrides tab files, and that app/properties files should be customized together with correct IDs/namespaces. It also covers merging/override rules for curam-config.xml, including global vs merged entries, address format overrides, and how to split config into -config.xml fragments. The section introduces key UI/system settings such as popup pages, error pages, multi-select, admin updates, static content delivery, field errors, security checks, select-all, transfer lists, conditional links, autocomplete, scrollbars, and pagination.

children.title: Custom resources
children.summary: Explains how to add custom resources to the web application by placing files in a component’s `WebContent` folder, which are copied directly into the generated web app root during client build. Notes that these files override existing generated resources, bypass normal merging/processing (for example CSS and JS properties), and can replace critical files like `struts-config.xml`, so caution is required. Also mentions copy order by component priority and timestamp, and gives the basic steps to create and populate the folder.

This section explains how to customize a Cúram web client without changing delivered components. It covers component order and build-time selection, adding new artifacts, overriding or merging same-named artifacts, and the special precedence of WebContent files. It also describes externalized strings, image references and image mapping, indirect links via CuramLinks.properties, runtime XML config files, login page, JavaScript and CSS customization, application/general configuration, and using custom resources copied directly into the generated WebContent root.

Overview of the Cúram web client and its UI model. Explains that applications are role-based collections of mostly UIM pages, with UIM XML defining page contents, limited layout, and server-interface bindings. Covers core UI structure (banner, sections/tabs, context panels, shortcuts, actions), page context and appearance limits, and how the client is launched via AppController.do. Also includes development/build and deployment topics: UIM-to-JSP generation, configuration, security, pagination/scrollbars, static content, and customization via WebContent overrides.

children.title: 3 Application configuration
3.1 Configuration files
children.summary: This section explains that applications, sections, tabs, and related UI elements are configured using XML files in the `<server-dir>\components\<component-name>\clientapps` directory. It introduces the main file types: `.app` files for defining an application, including its banner, referenced sections, and application search; and `.sec` files for defining section tabs and the section shortcut panel. It also notes that each file type has an associated schema describing supported attributes.

children.title: 3.2 Web client properties
Customizing the CDEJResources.properties file
children.summary: This section explains how to customize the CDEJResources.properties file used by the web client, including the browser tab title and other shared properties. It notes the file’s default location under the CuramCDEJ defaultproperties path, that it can be localized, and that images defined in it can also be localized. The customization procedure is simple: create a custom copy in the custom component (for example, webclient\components\custom) and include only the properties you want to override.

children.title: Configuring the browser title
children.summary: Explains how to customize the browser tab title via CDEJResources.Properties using `browser.tab.title`, `browser.tab.title.separator`, and `browser.tab.title.application.name.first`. Also introduces applications in the UI: an application is a role-specific view defined in a `.app` XML file under `clientapps`, with a banner showing the app name, user role, welcome message, menu links (preferences, help, about, logout), optional logo, and optional quick search.

Explains how to configure web client properties in CDEJResources.properties, especially the browser tab title. The file contains shared web client properties, is located under CuramCDEJ/doc/defaultproperties/curam/omega3/i18n, can be localized, and can include locale-specific images. To customize it, create a copy in the custom component (for example, webclient/components/custom) and include only overridden properties.

children.title: 3.3 Applications
Application definition
Application application-menu element
children.summary: Defines the application-menu element, part of the application banner, for adding up to two optional links: preferences and logout. The preferences link opens the user preferences dialog; the logout link ends the user session. Both child elements use a title attribute that references a properties file entry.

children.title: Application application-search element
children.summary: Defines the application-search element for application banners. Covers simple search via default-search-page and initial-text, noting these cannot be used with smart-navigator. For multiple search types, use search-pages containing one or more search-page entries with mandatory type, description, page-id, and initial-text, plus optional default; the selected search passes searchText and searchType to the UIM page. Also documents further-options-link for an advanced search page with description and page-id.

children.title: Application section-ref element
children.summary: Describes application configuration elements for Smart Navigator. The application must reference at least one and at most five sections using the mandatory section-ref element, whose id points to a .sec configuration file. It also explains the timeout-warning element for session timeout dialogs, including how it helps prevent data loss and its optional attributes: title, user-message, and quit-button, each typically backed by a properties file value.

children.title: Application timeout-warning element
children.summary: Explains the application-level timeout-warning element used to warn users before a session expires so they can save data and avoid losing input. Notes that the element is optional and uses default values if attributes are omitted. Describes configurable dialog text via properties-file references for the title, main user message, and quit button label. Also briefly notes that browser sessions time out based on server communication, and that users may otherwise be forced to reauthenticate and lose unsaved data.

children.title: Application context
children.summary: Explains the application context parameter used at the user application level to let infrastructure, context-aware tags, and renderers tailor output for a specific application, enabling reusable UIM pages with shared logic and different content. Shows how to set the context in the .app file, register matching values in the ApplicationContext code table, and how code table views return only context-specific codes when a context is active.

Defines the .app application file and banner configuration. Covers required section-ref links (1–5 sections), optional application-menu links for preferences/logout, application-search options for single or multiple search types plus advanced search, session timeout warning dialog settings, and application context for context-aware content and code table filtering. Also notes browser tab title properties and banner/logo-related attributes.

children.title: Application optional header
children.summary: Describes the optional application header configuration. You can replace or supplement the application banner by setting `header-type` and `header-source` on the `application` element. `header-type` must be `static` (XML/HTML fragment stored in the resource store with content type `text/xml`) or `dynamic` (a custom widget referenced via `styles-config.xml`). If the header replaces the banner, omit other optional application attributes. The header content, whether widget or fragment, must start with a `<div>` element.

children.title: Application example
children.summary: Shows a complete `SimpleApp.app` XML application configuration example. It defines app metadata, menu items (preferences, help, logout), search pages with default and alternate search criteria, a further options link, and section references. Also illustrates the required `SimpleApp.properties` file for localizable labels and messages, plus a note that the default logo path can be replaced with a custom image in the component’s Images folder.

children.title: Associate an application with a user
children.summary: Explains how to map a user to an application and initial home page using the Users table APPLICATIONCODE field, the APPLICATION_CODE codetable, and the application id. The code table value must match the .app filename, and the description specifies the UIM home page shown on login. Includes an example CT_APPLICATIONCODE.ctx entry and notes that the home page must be associated with the application. Ends by introducing Smart Navigator customization, including search targets, keywords, and Guice binding.

Defines .app application views for a user/role: banner/title/subtitle/welcome text, browser tab title properties, logo settings, optional header, application menu, quick search, timeout warning, and application context. Explains required section-ref links (minimum 1, maximum 5) and how search can be single-page, multi-type, or advanced, with Smart Navigator notes. Covers context-aware code tables via ApplicationContext. Includes a full SimpleApp.app example plus its properties file, and notes user-to-application mapping via APPLICATION_CODE.

children.title: 3.4 Customizing Cúram Smart Navigator
Enabling or disabling Cúram Smart Navigator
Enabling or disabling Cúram Smart Navigator by changing the .app files
children.summary: Explains how to enable or disable Cúram Smart Navigator by editing application `.app` files at build time, either per role or for all roles. Shows replacing the `application-search` element with `ac:smart-navigator` in files like `DefaultApp.app`, with customizable `initial-text` and `description` values. Also notes how to disable it by reversing the change and rebuilding. Includes a brief guide to creating new search targets via code table definitions, keyword binding, and Java implementation.

Explains how to enable or disable Cúram Smart Navigator per application role by editing the corresponding .app files at build time, such as replacing the application-search element in DefaultApp.app with ac:smart-navigator and setting optional initial-text and description values. Also covers disabling it by reversing the change and rebuilding. Briefly introduces the related process for creating new Smart Navigator search targets via code table definitions, keyword binding, and Java implementation.

children.title: Guide to creating a new search target
children.summary: Explains how to create a new Smart Navigator search target for navigating to application pages or tabs. The process has three steps: define the target in the parent SearchTarget code table, bind a keyword to that target, and implement the target in Java. Useful if you need to add a new navigable page/tab or understand the required code table and implementation pieces.

children.title: Creating search keywords
Creating search keywords by using the administration system
children.summary: Explains how to create Smart Navigator search keywords using the Cúram administration system instead of code tables. Covers the concept that keywords trigger search targets, that multiple keywords can map to one search target, and where to configure them in the admin UI: System Configurations > Shortcuts > Application Data > Code Tables. Provides steps to add a new keyword item, set its name and unique Technical ID, and publish it. Notes that this is the simpler method for linking keywords to search targets.

children.title: Creating search keywords by using code tables
children.summary: Explains how to create Smart Navigator search keywords using the SearchTarget and SearchTargetKeyword code tables. Covers adding a new keyword, linking it through the SearchKeywords hierarchy, publishing changes, and the need for matching locales. Includes the required CT_SearchTarget.ctx file naming limitation, and an example code snippet showing how parent_code, value, java_identifier, and locale-specific description fields bind keywords to search target implementations.

Explains how Smart Navigator search keywords are created and linked to search targets, either through the administration system or by code tables. Covers that keywords trigger search targets, multiple keywords can map to one target, and the admin UI path for adding/publishing keywords. Also describes the SearchTarget (parent) and SearchTargetKeyword (child) hierarchy, the CT_SearchTarget.ctx naming limitation, locale matching, and example fields like parent_code, value, java_identifier, and locale-specific description.

children.title: Creating search targets
children.summary: Explains how to create search targets for Cúram Smart Navigator. A search target is a predefined page/tab and must implement TargetObjectSearch with methods for searching, target type (REQUIRES_PERSON or NO_REQUIREMENT), SID authorization, and optional icon. Shows a simple Evidence Page example returning a single TargetObjectDtls result. Also notes that after creating keywords and the Java implementation, keywords must be bound to the search target so Smart Navigator recognizes it.

children.title: Binding keywords to search target implementations
children.summary: This section explains how to bind SearchTarget code table keywords to Java search target implementations using Guice injection. It shows creating a custom module that extends AbstractModule and uses a MapBinder<String, TargetObjectSearch> in configure(). An example binds SEARCHTARGET.CASE (the code table’s java_identifier) to PersonCaseSearch.class. It clarifies that this creates the Guice mapping between a SearchTarget category and its implementation, and includes the corresponding SearchTarget code table entry example.

children.title: Deleting or disabling keywords
children.summary: Explains how to delete or disable Smart Navigator search keywords and targets. Disabled keywords disappear dynamically from the KEYWORD list, while deleted keywords are removed only after a database rebuild. A search target can also be disabled by removing all its associated keywords. To delete keywords, remove the corresponding XML entries from CT_SearchTarget.ctx using a text editor, then rebuild the database.

children.title: Modifying keywords
Modifying keywords by using the administration system
children.summary: Explains how to modify Smart Navigator search target keywords through the administration system, which updates the keyword prompt dynamically. Also notes the limitation that database rebuilds revert changes, so permanent edits require updating CT_SearchTarget.ctx. The surrounding content covers disabling keywords and disabling search targets by hiding keywords or removing associated parent_code entries.

children.title: Modifying keywords in CT_SearchTarget.ctx
children.summary: Explains how to modify Smart Navigator Search Target keywords directly in CT_SearchTarget.ctx by editing the XML code table entries. Notes that the keyword shown to users is the field description, which can be changed to any text string, and that changes require rebuilding the database. Includes an example XML `<code>` entry and mentions that keyword translations can be provided for multiple locales, with runtime language chosen from the user’s locale.

This section explains how to modify Smart Navigator search target keywords, either through the administration system or by editing CT_SearchTarget.ctx directly. Administration changes update the keyword prompt dynamically, but database rebuilds reset them unless the XML is changed permanently. It also covers related management tasks: disabling keywords by hiding them in the code table, and disabling a search target by removing all linked keywords/parent_code entries.

children.title: Translating search targets and keywords
Translating search target descriptions
children.summary: Explains how to translate Smart Navigator search targets and search target keywords in Cúram. The section gives step-by-step admin procedures for finding the relevant code tables, adding translations, updating code table hierarchies, saving, and publishing changes. It also notes that translated values appear to caseworkers in the Search > KEYWORDS list.

children.title: Translating search target keywords
children.summary: Steps for translating Smart Navigator search target keywords in Cúram. As system administrator, open System Configurations > Shortcuts > Application Data > Code Tables, search for “Smart Navigator Search Target Keyword,” expand the table, use Translate to add a language-specific translation, then save. Next, go to Code Table Hierarchies > SearchKeywords, expand the keyword code table, change the parent code, save, and publish. The result is that the keyword translation appears in the KEYWORDS list for caseworkers.

Explains how Cúram system administrators translate Smart Navigator search target descriptions and keywords for multilingual support. Covers finding the relevant code tables under System Configurations > Shortcuts > Application Data > Code Tables, using Translate/Add Translation to enter locale-specific text, then updating the SearchKeywords code table hierarchy, saving, and publishing. Notes that translated values appear to caseworkers in Search > KEYWORDS. Also mentions keyword modification via CT_SearchTarget.ctx as an alternative to administration UI.

children.title: Overriding the person search
children.summary: Explains how to override person search in Cúram SmartNavigator. You extend curam.smartnavigator.target.impl.PersonSearchImpl and override optional methods for searching by terms and date of birth, by alternate ID and date of birth, or getSIDName. The default behavior can still be used for unmodified methods. After implementing the class, add a Guice binding to the custom key string custPerson (SmartNavigatorConstants.kPersonSearchKeyCustom) instead of a code table entry.

children.title: Customizing case search results
Overriding default filtering of case search results
Overriding the default case search results implementation
children.summary: Explains how to override default case search result filtering in Cúram Smart Navigator. You can supply a custom `curam.smartnavigator.core.util.SearchResultFilter` and implement `excludeResult(CaseHeaderDetails)` to hide cases from users. A Guice binding is required to register the custom class. It also describes extending `SearchResultFilterImpl` to change the SID name mapping by overriding `getSIDName(...)`, where unmapped or unauthorized SIDs are filtered out.

children.title: Extending the default search results implementation
children.summary: This section explains how to customize default case search result filtering in Smart Navigator. You can either implement the SearchResultFilter interface to exclude cases via excludeResult(CaseHeaderDetails), or extend the default SearchResultFilterImpl to change how SID names are derived from case type codes by overriding getSIDName(CaseHeaderDetails). It also shows that Guice bindings are used to register the custom implementation.

This section explains how to customize Smart Navigator case search result filtering. By default, results from reference number or case keyword searches are filtered using `SearchResultFilterImpl`, which removes unauthorized cases and unmapped case types based on SID-to-case-type mappings. It describes two options: implement `SearchResultFilter` and override `excludeResult(CaseHeaderDetails)` to hide cases, or extend `SearchResultFilterImpl` and override `getSIDName(...)` to change SID mapping. It also shows registering the custom class with a Guice binding.

children.title: Overriding default descriptions for case search results
children.summary: Explains how to override the default case search result description in Smart Navigator. Case search results display a case name next to the reference number, supplied by `curam.smartnavigator.core.util.CaseTypeDescriptionResolver`. To customize it, implement this interface and its `determineProductTypeDescription(CaseIDAndTypeKey key)` method, which returns the description used in search results. The displayed format is `ProductTypeDescription, referenceNumber` (for example, `Application, 257`).

Explains how to customize Smart Navigator case search results for reference number and case keyword searches. Covers overriding default filtering via `SearchResultFilter` or extending `SearchResultFilterImpl` to change SID-to-case-type mapping, including the key methods and Guice binding/module registration in `MODULCLASSNAME.dmx`. Also describes overriding result labels by implementing `CaseTypeDescriptionResolver` so search results display a custom product type description plus reference number, e.g., `Application, 257`.

children.title: Modifying search targets redirect URLs
children.summary: Explains how to change Smart Navigator search target redirect URLs by editing a property value in Property Administration. Steps include logging in as a system administrator, navigating to System Configurations > Shortcuts > Application Data > Property Administration, searching for “smart,” editing the relevant URL property (for example, curam.smartnavigator.person.evidence.url), saving, and publishing. Useful when you want search target pages to redirect to a different URL, such as a person’s eligibility page.

children.title: Setting the preferred tabs by populating the attribute preferredTabs
children.summary: Explains how to set Smart Navigator’s preferred tabs by populating the preferredTabs attribute on TargetObjectSearch in a search target implementation. Use this only when the target page is configured on more than one tab; you can specify one tab or an ordered list of preferred tabs. Includes a Java example setting preferredTabs to “ProspectPersonHome,” plus a note that searching after the change should return content in the updated tabs. Also points to page-to-tab and tab-to-section associations for related configuration.

children.title: Enabling or disabling recent searches
children.summary: Explains how to configure Smart Navigator recent search history via the `curam.smartnavigator.search.history.threshold` property. The section gives admin steps to set recent searches to none (`0`), a fixed number (`X`), or all (`-1`), then publish changes. It notes the default behavior shows the last six searches and that users can view them by clicking in the search box. Also begins a related section on setting the search debounce timeout (default 500 ms) and disabling search-as-you-type.

children.title: Setting the debounce timeout
children.summary: This section explains how Smart Navigator search is delayed while users type. It says the default debounce timeout is 500 ms, meaning results appear only after typing stops for half a second. It also notes that search-as-you-type can be disabled entirely, so searches run only when the user presses Enter or clicks the search icon. The section begins the procedure for changing this setting through Property Administration as a system administrator.

children.title: Implementing a navigation hook
children.summary: Describes how to implement a Smart Navigator navigation hook to intercept direct navigation from search results. The hook lets you add custom JavaScript business logic between clicking a result and opening the target page, useful when bypassing intermediate pages is not desired. Example use case: show a modal with important information before navigation. This section signals step-by-step instructions for providing the hook implementation.

Covers how to customize Cúram Smart Navigator: enable/disable it per application role via `.app` files, create and bind new search targets and keywords with code tables/Guice, modify or delete keywords, translate them, override person and case search behavior and results, change redirect URLs, set preferred tabs, control recent-search history and debounce timing, and implement a navigation hook to intercept result clicks.

children.title: 3.5 Sections
Section definition
Section tab element
children.summary: Defines the section tab element used to associate tabs with a section. A section is a collection of tabs, must include at least one tab, and each tab may be referenced by only one section within an application; tabs can be reused only across separate applications. The tab element has one mandatory attribute, id, which points to a .tab configuration file. The excerpt also notes the related shortcut-panel-ref element, which allows one optional section shortcut panel per section via a mandatory id to a .sec file.

children.title: Section shortcut-panel-ref element
children.summary: Defines the `shortcut-panel-ref` element for a section. It lets you attach a section shortcut panel to a section and only one `shortcut-panel-ref` is allowed per section. The only required attribute is `id`, which must reference a section shortcut panel (`.sec`) file. The excerpt also notes that `section` elements can include tabs and a shortcut panel reference, with tabs requiring at least one `tab` and each tab used by only one section within an application.

Defines the `.sec` section configuration file and the `section` root element. Covers required attributes `id` and `title`, optional `hide-tab-container` and `default-page-id`, and notes `default-page-id` cannot be used for the Home/first section. Describes child elements: `tab` (1..n, each referencing one `.tab` file and usable by only one section per application) and optional `shortcut-panel-ref` (one per section, referencing a `.sec` shortcut panel file).

children.title: Section example
children.summary: Shows an example of a section configuration file (`SimpleWorkspaceSection.sec`) and its matching properties file for localizable text. It illustrates a section containing a shortcut panel reference and tabs. The section shortcut panel is then introduced: it can provide quick links and actions, be organized into categories, starts collapsed by default, and uses ellipses to mark modal-dialog actions. It also explains that shortcut panels are defined in `.ssp` XML files in `clientapps`, with a mandatory `id` matching the filename.

Describes how an application section is configured in a `.sec` XML file. A section groups 1..n tabs and may include one shortcut panel. Covers mandatory section attributes `id` and `title`, optional `hide-tab-container` and `default-page-id` (not allowed for the Home/first section), and notes `id` must match the filename and application references. Explains the `tab` child (each tab can belong to only one section per application) and optional `shortcut-panel-ref` (one per section).

children.title: 3.6 Section shortcut panel
Section shortcut panel definition
Section shortcut panel node element
children.summary: Defines the `node` element used inside a section shortcut panel to represent menu items and categories. It supports three types: `group` for categories containing `leaf` and `separator` children, `leaf` for menu items that open a page or modal dialog (ellipsis shown for modals), and `separator` for spacing between items. Key attributes are `id` (mandatory, unique within the `.ssp` file) and `type` (mandatory). Useful for configuring shortcut panel structure and navigation behavior.

Defines the XML structure for a section shortcut panel (.ssp) used by a section to provide quick links to pages, actions, and modal dialogs. Covers the root `section-shortcut-panel` element, its mandatory `id` and `title` attributes, and the required `nodes` child container. Explains `node` types: `group` for categories, `leaf` for menu items that open pages or modals (shown with ellipsis), and `separator` for spacing. Useful for configuring shortcut panel navigation and hierarchy.

children.title: Section shortcut panel example
children.summary: Describes section shortcut panel node attributes and an example `.ssp` definition. Key attributes include `title` (required, from properties file), `page-id` for leaf nodes, `open-as="modal"` for modal dialogs, and `append-ellipsis` to control the modal ellipsis. The example `SimpleShortcutPanel.ssp` shows grouped nodes like Searches, QuickLinks, and Registration, with leaf items such as `PersonSearch` and `RegisterEmployer`, plus a separator node.

Explains how a section can define an optional shortcut panel in a `.ssp` XML file under `clientapps` for quick links to pages, actions, and modal dialogs. Covers the root `section-shortcut-panel` element with mandatory `id` and `title`, plus required `nodes`. Describes node types: `group` categories, `leaf` menu items that open pages or modals, and `separator` spacing. Also details key leaf attributes like `page-id`, `open-as="modal"`, and `append-ellipsis`, with a worked example `SimpleShortcutPanel.ssp`.

children.title: 3.7 Tabs
Tab definition
Tab page-param element
children.summary: Explains the `page-param` element for tabs: it lets you define multiple page parameters, each mapped to a name-value pair passed to UIM pages opened from the tab actions menu and navigation bar. These parameters also uniquely identify tab instances, such as separate person tabs by `id`. Includes the `name` attribute as mandatory and unique. The section then briefly introduces the tab actions menu `menu` element and the mandatory tab `context` element, noting that a context panel UIM page can populate tab title/name or be omitted if title/name are specified directly.

children.title: Tab actions menu element
children.summary: This section explains the tab actions menu element, which links a tab to a separate .mnu configuration file containing tab-specific actions. It notes that the menu element has a mandatory id attribute referencing the menu configuration file, and that these actions can be shown from the tab title bar. The surrounding text also covers page-param usage for passing name-value pairs to UIM pages and uniquely identifying tab instances, plus a brief introduction to the context element for defining tab context panels.

children.title: Tab context element
children.summary: This section explains the tab context element, which defines a context panel by referencing a UIM page. The context element is mandatory; if no context panel is used, the tab must instead specify a tab name and tab title. It also notes that tab name and title can be populated either from the context panel UIM page or from tab-name/tab-title attributes in the .tab file. If the UIM page is only used to supply name/title data, set its height to zero.

children.title: Tab navigation element
children.summary: Defines the tab navigation element, which controls what pages open within a tab. It can point to a single UIM page via page-id or to multiple pages via a .nav tab navigation file via id. The navigation element is mandatory, and one of page-id or id must be specified. It also explains that tab navigation groups UIM pages within a tab and uses the Content Area Navigation Bar and Page Group Navigation Bar components.

children.title: Tab smart-panel element
children.summary: Describes the tab smart-panel element and tab-refresh behavior. The smart panel content is a UIM page set by mandatory page-id and title attributes; width defaults to 250px and collapsed defaults to true. It notes the smart panel is limited to certain UIM elements and is related to context panels. The tab-refresh element controls refreshing of the tab actions menu, tab navigation, and context panel when a specific UIM page loads or a page is submitted from a modal or content area.

children.title: Tab tab-refresh element
Context panel refresh example
children.summary: Explains how to configure context panel refresh so it updates automatically when a modal/page is submitted. The example uses the Administration Workspace > Tabs > Refresh Options, then enables On Submit with Refresh Context for a specific page ID, resulting in updated outstanding verifications appearing without manual refresh. Also introduces context panel UIM: a PAGE with TYPE="DETAILS" that supports only a limited subset of UIM elements, including SERVER_INTERFACE in DISPLAY phase and ACTION_CONTROL in ACTION type, with some elements unsupported.

Defines the tab-refresh element, which controls refreshing a tab’s actions menu, navigation, context panel, and sometimes main content. Refresh can be triggered when a specific UIM page is loaded or submitted from a modal/content area. By default, only tab content refreshes on modal submit; context panel, menu, and navigation must be explicitly configured. Child elements onload and onsubmit take page-id plus optional context, menu-bar, navigation, and main-content flags. Main-content refresh is only for modals opened from nested list drop-down panels.

Defines a `.tab` file and its root `tab-config` element, which must have an `id` matching the filename. Covers mandatory/optional child elements: `page-param` for passing name-value pairs and uniquely identifying tab instances, `menu` for tab actions from a `.mnu` file, `context` for the context panel or tab name/title, `navigation` for single-page or multi-page tab navigation, `smart-panel` for an optional side panel, and `tab-refresh` for event-based refreshing of menu, navigation, context, and sometimes main content.

children.title: Context panel UIM
children.summary: Defines a context panel UIM as a PAGE with TYPE="DETAILS" and limited element support. Only SERVER_INTERFACE in DISPLAY phase and ACTION_CONTROL with ACTION type are allowed; other UIM elements are unsupported. The surrounding page also describes configuring context panel refresh options in Admin for tabs/evidence submission so the panel updates automatically after a modal submit, plus a related note on dynamically enabling or hiding tab actions.

children.title: Tab example configuration file
children.summary: Shows a sample tab configuration file (`SimpleTab.tab`) and its XML structure. Covers key elements such as `tc:tab-config`, `tc:page-param`, `tc:menu`, `tc:context` with `tab-name`, `tc:navigation`, `tc:smart-panel`, and `tc:tab-refresh` with `onload` and `onsubmit` actions. Also notes that context panel UIM pages require mandatory `TAB_NAME` and `PAGE_TITLE` elements, and references their definitions for dynamic tab text and page titles.

Explains tab configuration in `.tab` files. A tab uses root `tab-config` with `id` matching the filename and can define `page-param` values, an actions `menu`, mandatory `context`, mandatory `navigation`, optional `smart-panel`, and optional `tab-refresh`. Describes how tabs represent business objects, support tab/page-group navigation, dynamic show/hide/disable behavior, and refresh on load/submit. Also covers context panel UIM restrictions (`PAGE TYPE="DETAILS"`, limited elements), required `TAB_NAME` and `PAGE_TITLE`, and includes a sample `SimpleTab.tab` configuration.

children.title: 3.8 Tab actions menus
Tab actions menu definition and menu-bar element
Tab actions menu menu-item element
children.summary: Defines the tab actions menu’s `menu-item` element and its attributes. Menu items can open a UIM page in the tab content area, open a modal dialog, or download a file. Key attributes include mandatory `id`, `page-id`, and `title`; optional `open-as=modal`, `append-ellipsis` for modal items, `window-options` for dialog size, `dynamic`, `visible`, `type=FILE_DOWNLOAD`, and `description`. Also notes that modal items are shown with an ellipsis by default.

children.title: Tab actions menu submenu element
children.summary: This section defines the tab actions menu submenu element. It explains that a submenu groups menu items, must have mandatory id and title attributes, and can optionally include a description, all sourced from a properties file. It also describes supported child elements: menu-item for UIM page links, nested submenu for deeper grouping, and menu-separator for visual division. The section begins the related menu-separator description for separating submenu entries.

children.title: Tab actions menu menu-separator element
children.summary: Defines the tab actions menu separator element. A menu-separator adds a line separator in a tab actions menu or its submenus to divide menu entries. It is a supported child of submenu and can be used 0..n times. The section introduces the element and indicates that its attributes are described in the following table.

children.title: Tab actions menu loader-registry element
children.summary: Describes the tab actions menu `loader-registry` XML element, which holds one or more `loader` entries used to dynamically enable/disable or show/hide tab menu items. Notes that `loader-registry` supports `loader` children, and each `loader` requires a `class` attribute naming a concrete implementation of `curam.util.tab.impl.DynamicMenuStateLoader`. Also points to related dynamic tab actions support and explains this works with the `menu-item` dynamic attribute.

children.title: Tab actions menu loader element
children.summary: Defines the tab actions menu loader element, which specifies a single Java loader implementation used to dynamically set menu item visibility and enabled/disabled state. The only supported attribute is mandatory class, the fully qualified name of a curam.util.tab.impl.DynamicMenuStateLoader implementation. It also references the loader-registry element, which contains one or more loader elements for dynamic tab actions support.

Defines tab actions menus in a .mnu file with root `menu-bar` and optional `max-inline-items` for inline tab title-bar actions. Covers supported children: `menu-item` (open UIM page in tab content, modal dialog, or file download; key attrs `id`, `page-id`, `title`, plus `open-as=modal`, `append-ellipsis`, `window-options`, `dynamic`, `visible`, `type=FILE_DOWNLOAD`, `description`), `submenu` for grouping, `menu-separator` for dividers, and `loader-registry` with `loader` entries for dynamic show/hide or enable/disable behavior.

children.title: Tab actions menu dynamic support
children.summary: Explains how tab action menu items can be dynamically enabled, disabled, hidden, or shown using `dynamic="true"` on `menu-item`, a `loader-registry`, and a Java `DynamicMenuStateLoader` implementation. Describes the `loadMenuState` method inputs and requirement to return state for all dynamic items. Also notes tab refresh options from the `.tab` file and introduces `FILE_DOWNLOAD` menu items, including `page-id` matching and use of tab-defined parameters.

children.title: File download menu item
children.summary: Explains how to configure a file download menu item in curam-config.xml using a menu-item with type="FILE_DOWNLOAD" and a matching page-id. Notes that only tab-defined parameters can be used as PAGE_PARAM values for INPUT elements, so required values like fileID must be declared in the tab configuration (.tab). Also briefly ties this to dynamic tab actions support via menu-item dynamic attributes and DynamicMenuStateLoader, which can hide or disable menu items at load time.

children.title: Tab actions menu example configuration file
children.summary: Shows an example tab actions menu configuration file, `SimpleMenu.mnu`, for a Cúram tab menu. The example defines a `menu-bar` with a loader registry, a `Person` submenu, a dynamic menu item, a separator, a simple page link, and a modal menu item with window options. It also notes that the `.mnu` file requires a matching `SimpleMenu.properties` file for localizable titles.

Defines tab actions menus in .mnu files for tab title-bar actions. Covers the root menu-bar element, optional max-inline-items for inline items, and supported children: menu-item, submenu, menu-separator, and loader-registry. Menu items can open UIM pages in-tab or in a modal (ellipsis shown), or download files via type="FILE_DOWNLOAD". Explains dynamic hide/show/enable/disable via dynamic="true" plus DynamicMenuStateLoader, and notes FILE_DOWNLOAD page-id and tab-parameter constraints. Includes a sample SimpleMenu.mnu with matching properties file.

children.title: 3.9 Tab navigation
Tab navigation definition
Tab navigation nodes element
children.summary: Describes the tab navigation nodes element, which groups navigation tabs and navigation pages. It supports child elements navigation-page (tabs without a page group bar) and navigation-group (tabs with a page group navigation bar). For navigation-group, the section details required and optional attributes: id and title are mandatory; dynamic, visible, and description are optional. It also notes title and description reference entries in the associated properties file, and that dynamic tabs can be disabled or hidden.

children.title: Tab navigation navigation-group element
children.summary: Defines the `navigation-group` element for tab navigation. It represents a navigation tab with a page group navigation bar and groups `navigation-page` elements under it. Key attributes are `id` (unique, mandatory), `title` (mandatory, properties-file reference), `dynamic` (optional boolean to allow disabling/hiding at runtime), `visible` (optional boolean, default `true`), and `description` (optional admin-only text, properties-file reference).

children.title: Tab navigation navigation-page element
children.summary: Defines the `navigation-page` element used for both navigation tabs and page-group navigation pages. It explains that when placed under `nodes`, it creates a navigation tab in the navigation bar; when under `navigation-group`, it creates a navigation page in the page group navigation bar. Key attributes are `id`, `page-id`, and `title` (all mandatory), plus optional `dynamic` and `visible`. It also notes that the first page in a `navigation-group` is shown initially, and later selections remember the last chosen page.

children.title: Tab navigation loader-registry element
children.summary: Defines the tab navigation `loader-registry` element for dynamic navigation support. It holds 1..n `loader` child elements, each specifying a loader implementation used to enable/disable or show/hide navigation tabs and navigation pages. This section also introduces the related `loader` element, whose required `class` attribute must name a `curam.util.tab.impl.DynamicNavStateLoader` implementation.

children.title: Tab navigation loader element
children.summary: Defines the tab navigation loader-registry and loader elements for dynamic tab/page visibility and enabled state. loader-registry contains one or more loader implementations. Each loader specifies a mandatory fully qualified class name implementing curam.util.tab.impl.DynamicNavStateLoader. Includes an optional description attribute for administration only, referenced from the properties file. Useful for configuring dynamic enable/disable and show/hide behavior of navigation tabs and page groups.

Defines tab navigation configuration in a .nav XML file under clientapps. The root navigation element must include id matching the file name and supports nodes (required) plus optional loader-registry for dynamic state changes. nodes contains navigation-page tabs without a page group bar and navigation-group tabs with grouped pages. navigation-group and navigation-page both use mandatory id, title, and page-id where applicable; dynamic, visible, and description are optional. It also explains initial page selection, remembered page state, and dynamic enable/disable or show/hide support via loader implementations.

children.title: Tab navigation dynamic support
children.summary: Explains how tab and page group navigation can be dynamically enabled, disabled, hidden, or shown. Dynamic support uses the `dynamic="true"` attribute on `navigation-page`/`navigation-group`, plus a `loader-registry` entry pointing to a Java class implementing `curam.util.tab.impl.DynamicNavStateLoader`. The loader is invoked when the tab first loads (based on tab refresh options) and must return a state for all dynamic identifiers using page parameters. Identifiers must be unique within the nav file.

children.title: Tab navigation example configuration file
children.summary: Shows an example tab navigation configuration file, `SimpleNavigation.nav`, including the XML structure for navigation pages, a navigation group, and a dynamic page, plus the matching `SimpleNavigation.properties` labels for localizable titles. Also explains how sections and tabs can be opened: directly via their tab controls or via links, and that opening a new tab can switch to another section. Useful for understanding tab navigation setup and interaction behavior.

Defines tab navigation in `.nav` files under `clientapps`: a `navigation` root whose `id` matches the file name, with mandatory `nodes` and optional `loader-registry`. `nodes` contains either `navigation-page` tabs or `navigation-group` tabs with page groups; each item needs unique `id`, `title`, and `page-id` where applicable, plus optional `dynamic`, `visible`, and `description`. Explains first-page selection, remembered page state, and dynamic hide/disable behavior via `DynamicNavStateLoader` loaded on tab refresh.

children.title: 3.10 Opening tabs and sections
Using links to open tabs and sections
children.summary: Explains how selecting links or other action controls can open UIM pages as new tabs or sections. Describes the contexts where UIM pages appear (content area, tab context/smart panel, modal dialog, list dropdown panel) and the link types that can trigger navigation (page actions, modal buttons, buttons, hyperlinked text, list actions, tab actions, shortcut panel links, nav bar tabs, page group entries). Notes that the client decides before opening whether the target should open in a new tab or section based on configuration.

children.title: Page to tab and tab to section associations
children.summary: Explains how Cúram maps pages to tabs and tabs to sections. A tab’s navigation is defined in the .tab file and optionally a .nav file; referenced page-id UIM pages are associated with that tab and open it when linked. Page-to-tab mapping must be unique, with limited exceptions for context-only reuse across tabs or different .app configurations. Reusing .nav files across apps is allowed. Advises against using resolve pages in navigation. Tabs are associated with sections via the .sec file, and opening a link focuses the tab’s section.

children.title: Tab and section page parameters
children.summary: Explains how tab/page parameters control tab opening behavior. The client checks the selected link’s page-to-tab association, existing open tabs, and passed parameter values to decide whether to reuse the current tab or open a new instance. Same tab configuration can open multiple instances when identifying page parameters differ, such as different concern role IDs. Parameter names must exactly match the tab configuration; missing required parameters make behavior undefined, and extra parameters are ignored.

children.title: Tab ordering
children.summary: Describes tab ordering for new tabs: by default, new tabs open at the end of the tab list, but the application property `curam.environment.enable.sequential.tabs` can change this so tabs open next to the tab that launched them. The default value is `false`. Also notes this in the context of page-to-tab behavior and upcoming actions menu configuration.

Explains how links and tab controls open tabs and sections, including where UIM pages can appear and which action types can trigger navigation. Describes page-to-tab and tab-to-section mapping via .tab/.nav/.sec files, uniqueness limits and exceptions, and warns against resolve pages in navigation. Covers how page parameters and existing open tabs determine whether a link reuses the current tab or opens a new instance, and notes tab ordering behavior, including the sequential-tabs application property.

children.title: 3.11 Tab, page, and list actions menus
(Deprecated) Enabling inline menu items for actions menus
children.summary: Deprecated 8.0.3 procedure for enabling standardized inline items in actions menus, with separate application properties for tabs, pages, and lists. Describes default behavior when disabled (tabs: all overflow; lists: all overflow; pages: up to two inline, otherwise overflow) and what changes when enabled: first two tab/page actions inline, first list action inline, rest in overflow. Includes admin steps in Property Administration and notes server restart is required; from 8.1+ these properties are no longer needed.

children.title: Configuring inline menu items for actions menus
children.summary: Explains how to configure the default number of inline items in actions menus at the application level for tabs, pages, and lists. Requires inline action menu items to be enabled first. Documents the three properties, curam.actionmenus.max-inline-items.tab/page/list, their scope, recommended inline counts (tabs/pages 2–3, max 4; lists 1–2, max 2), and that setting 0 moves all actions to the overflow menu. Includes the sysadmin procedure via Property Administration > Infrastructure - Action Menu Parameters.

children.title: Customizing inline menu items for individual actions menus
children.summary: Explains how to customize the number of inline items for specific actions menus when inline actions are enabled. Covers tab actions menus in .mnu menu-bar files using max-inline-items, and page/list actions menus in UIM ACTION_SET elements using MAX_INLINE_ITEMS. Includes examples showing 3 tab actions, 4 page actions, and 2 list row actions, with guidance to order the most important actions first. Notes that changing one menu does not affect others and that rebuilding the application is required.

Explains tab, page, and list actions menus in Cúram 8.1.2. Covers standardized inline vs overflow behavior, default inline counts (tabs/pages 2, lists 1), best practices for ordering and label design, and how to configure inline actions application-wide via properties. Also shows how to override counts for specific tab (.mnu) and page/list UIM ACTION_SET menus, with examples and build/restart requirements. Includes deprecated 8.0.3 enablement steps and notes that 8.1+ has standardized behavior by default.

Covers web client application configuration via XML files in `clientapps` and related schemas: `.app` applications, `.sec` sections, `.ssp` shortcut panels, `.tab` tabs, `.nav` navigation, and `.mnu` actions menus. Explains browser title properties in `CDEJResources.properties`, application banners/search/context, section/tab mapping, opening tabs and sections, Smart Navigator customization, and standardized inline actions for tab/page/list menus, including application-wide and per-menu overrides. Includes validation/build processing and key XML attribute requirements.

children.title: 4 Localization
4.1 Numbers
children.summary: Explains how Cúram handles numbers during localization: numbers are treated in a locale-specific way based on the user’s preferred language, so formats like 7,99 vs 7.99 vary by locale. This section is a brief overview within the localization chapter and points readers to the broader file-encoding guidance that follows. Useful if you need to understand language-dependent numeric formatting in translated applications.

children.title: 4.2 File encoding
children.summary: Explains file encoding requirements for localized Cúram applications. XML files must declare encoding in the first line (typically UTF-8), and the declaration must match the actual saved encoding. UTF-8 is strongly recommended; editors may or may not update the file encoding automatically. Also notes that Java properties files and other non-XML files have different encoding handling, with client properties files assuming an encoding based on the default locale.

children.title: 4.3 Locales
Non-JavaScript property files
children.summary: Explains how to localize non-JavaScript property files by creating one .properties file per locale, naming locale-specific files with an underscore and locale code (for example, SomeFile_es.properties), with fallback to the default file if a key is missing. Notes that LOCALE_LIST must be updated for client builds and that merged properties behave like default locale files. Also briefly covers JavaScript property file localization (*.js.properties), including locale-specific naming and fallback, and introduces the language toggle for internal users’ default locale selection.

children.title: JavaScript property files
children.summary: Explains how to localize JavaScript property files by creating locale-specific `.js.properties` files (for example, `SomeJSFile.js_es.properties`) alongside the default `*.js.properties`. Locale suffixes use underscore plus locale identifier, and missing properties fall back to the default file. The section also notes that these files are automatically processed in a client build.

Defines locale handling in Cúram using Java locale identifiers in properties files. A locale has a required language code and optional parts separated by underscores, e.g. `en` or `en_US`. Locale-specific application data is stored mainly in properties files, and file naming uses locale suffixes to support localized variants and fallback behavior. Useful for understanding how Cúram identifies and selects locale-specific resources.

children.title: 4.4 Language toggle
children.summary: Explains the language toggle feature for internal users: administrators can change an internal user’s default locale to alter the language of field labels, tabs, and shortcut menu items. The application shows the user’s language in their default locale, and admins can select from all enabled locale codes in the Locale code table. It warns to enable only locale codes for languages actually installed in the application.

children.title: 4.5 Localizable text translations
Fallback for localizable text
children.summary: Explains Cúram’s fallback mechanism for localizable text when a user’s locale translation is missing. It prioritizes user locale, user language, default server locale, server language, then blank, using Java ResourceBundle. Migrated legacy fields add one more fallback step before blank, returning legacy text if present. Also covers how administrators edit translations via Add Translations or the Edit screen, which shows the best available translation and creates a new locale-specific translation without overwriting existing ones.

Explains Cúram localizable text translations: how to model text as a Localizable Text entity, shortText vs richText attributes, and how translations are stored per locale. Describes the locale fallback order when a user’s translation is missing (user locale, language, default server locale, language, blank), plus an extra legacy-text fallback for migrated fields. Also covers administrator editing via Add Translations or the Edit screen, which shows the best available translation and creates a new locale-specific translation without overwriting existing ones.

children.title: 4.6 UIM externalized strings
children.summary: UIM file strings must be externalized into .properties files named to match the .uim/.vim file and stored in the same folder. Properties from components are merged in component order, then page/view order; page properties override view properties even across component priority. UIM pages, views, and JavaScript can reference externalized strings. This section also points to related localization guidance and introduces JavaScript externalized strings, including .js.properties naming and merge rules.

children.title: 4.7 JavaScript externalized strings
Accessing properties in JavaScript
children.summary: Explains how to access JavaScript properties in Cúram using localized resources. The section shows a 3-step process: load resources with `dojo.requireLocalization()`, create a `curam.util.ResourceBundle`, then call `getProperty()` for normal or substituted values. It notes that `curam.application` is the default package and `SomeJSFile` comes from the JavaScript properties file name. Also mentions placeholder rules for property values (`%1s`, `%2s`, etc.) and briefly covers localization behavior for `Image.properties`.

JavaScript string values must be externalized into matching `.js.properties` files, named after the `.js` file (for example, `SomeJSFile.js.properties`). These files are usually stored alongside the script, except `.js` files under `WebContent`, whose properties file must be outside that directory. Matching property files across components are merged, with higher-priority components overriding duplicate property names. This section is about the naming, placement, and merge behavior for localized JavaScript resources.

children.title: 4.8 Image.properties
children.summary: Image.properties is localized like other properties files: place localized versions in the same directory as the base file. If a property is missing in a localized file, the default-locale file is used. The section also reiterates JavaScript property access via dojo.requireLocalization(), curam.util.ResourceBundle, and getProperty() with substitution values, and explains placeholder syntax in property values (%1s, %2s, etc.) with numbering limited to the number of placeholders.

children.title: 4.9 Infrastructure widget properties files
Frequency Pattern Selector localization
children.summary: Explains how to localize the Frequency Pattern Selector widget by using fixed placeholders in FrequencyPatternSelector.properties to build whole frequency-pattern sentences across languages. Notes that placeholder order controls widget layout, so translations may reorder values but cannot add, change, or remove placeholders. Lists placeholder meanings, including %dayInterval%, %weekInterval%, %dayOfWeek%, and %dayOfWeekExtended%, which map to inputs, checkboxes, and dropdowns for frequency pattern selection.

Lists Infrastructure widget properties files and explains customization rules: reserved filenames for widgets like AgendaPlayer, Calendar, FrequencyPatternSelector, etc., which must not be reused for other client properties files. To customize, copy the sample file from <cdej-dir>/doc/defaultproperties/ into webclient/components/custom. Also details Frequency Pattern Selector localization: sentences are built from fixed placeholders in FrequencyPatternSelector.properties, and placeholder order controls widget layout. Placeholders cannot be added, removed, or changed; examples include %dayInterval%, %weekInterval%, %dayOfWeek%, and %dayOfWeekExtended%.

children.title: 4.10 CDEJResources.properties
children.summary: Describes the localizable CDEJResources.properties file for widget text and images. Focuses on fixed placeholders used by the Frequency Pattern Selector and maps each property name to its required placeholder set (daily, weekly, monthly, bimonthly, yearly variants). Notes that placeholders must be used exactly when localizing widget properties, and that this file’s images can also be customized per locale.

children.title: 4.11 ApplicationConfiguration.properties
children.summary: ApplicationConfiguration.properties is generally not localized, but it contains a few localization-related settings for date and address formatting. The section points readers to the broader “Application configuration properties” topic for details.

children.title: 4.12 Application menu
children.summary: Explains how to localize the application menu in the application banner. Menu items are defined in curam-config.xml; the LINK element’s LABEL should normally be set to a key in CDEJResources.properties, with the same key/value provided in each localized properties file. For non-translated apps, direct text can be used in LABEL, but this is not suitable for multiple languages. References the general locales/properties-file localization process.

children.title: 4.13 Tabbed configuration artifacts
children.summary: Tabbed configuration artifacts use a companion properties file for any localizable text. To translate or localize the text for a specific language, place the locale-specific properties file in the same directory as the associated tabbed configuration artifact within the <custom> component. These properties files follow the standard locale rules described in the locales section.

children.title: 4.14 Runtime messages
children.summary: Explains how to localize or customize CDEJ runtime messages by creating RuntimeMessages.properties in a client component folder, with locale-specific variants named per Java properties conventions (for example, RuntimeMessages_es.properties). Custom messages override default CDEJ messages, with locale fallbacks applied and custom catalogs checked first. Only changed messages need to be copied. Message placeholders (%n) must remain unchanged, though their order may be rearranged.

Cúram localization covers locale-sensitive numbers, file encoding rules, and locale naming/fallback for properties files. It explains language toggling for internal users, localizable text translation storage and fallback, and how to externalize strings for UIM pages, JavaScript, images, widgets, menus, tabbed artifacts, and runtime messages. Also includes special rules for infrastructure widget properties and frequency pattern placeholders. Useful if you need guidance on translating or customizing any locale-dependent resource in the application.

children.title: 5 Session management
5.1 Session Overview
children.summary: Describes browser session handling in Cúram: sessions start on login, end on logout, browser shutdown, or after inactivity timeout (default 30 minutes, configurable). Also covers per-application-section tab limits: default 15 open tabs, configurable via `curam.environment.max.open.tabs`. When the limit is reached, an informational dialog appears; additional tabs are blocked with an error dialog until tabs are closed. Open tabs are restored after logout/login and browser refresh, with noted exceptions.

children.title: 5.2 Tab Restoration
children.summary: Explains how tab restoration works in Cúram 8.1.2. Open tabs are stored in web-tier session data and persisted intermittently to the database, so recent tab changes may be lost after timeout or browser restart. Distinguishes browser refresh (restores current session state, keeps current section’s selected tab) from new session/login (restores database state, Home becomes selected). Notes that shortcut/smart/page contents panel states are not restored, and changed tab limits or removed tabs affect what can be restored.

children.title: 5.3 Session Configuration
children.summary: Configures when tab session data is persisted from the web tier to the database via ApplicationConfiguration.properties. Two thresholds control writes: tabSessionUpdateCountThreshold (default 10 updates; 1 = every update; 0/invalid disables) and tabSessionUpdatePeriodThreshold (default 120 seconds; 0/invalid disables). Writes occur when either threshold is reached first; if count is 1, period is ignored. Logout normally persists data unless both thresholds are 0. Notes risks with shared logins and recommends disabling persistence in testing or shared external-user environments.

children.title: 5.4 Session timeout warning
Session timeout warning default values
children.summary: Explains how browser session timeout warnings work and where their defaults are configured. The modal appears before timeout with a countdown, letting users continue or quit; if the session has already expired, reauthentication may be required and data can be lost. Default text values come from CDEJResources.properties (title, pre-expiry message, expired message, Continue/Quit labels), and modal width defaults to 580 px in ApplicationConfiguration.properties, overridable via the application’s timeout-warning element.

children.title: Customizing the session timeout warning in the caseworker application
children.summary: Explains how to customize the session timeout warning in internal apps like the caseworker application. Covers using system application properties and CDEJ resource properties, noting CDEJ settings apply systemwide and app-specific timeout-warning element settings override both. Includes admin steps to edit and publish properties. Key properties: curam.environment.internal.enable.timeout.warning.modal to enable/disable the warning (default true) and curam.environment.internal.timeout.warning.modal.time to set the notice period in seconds (default 120). Also mentions modal height and buffering period defaults.

children.title: Customizing the session timeout warning in Universal Access
children.summary: Explains how to customize Universal Access session timeout warnings via system application properties and CDEJ resource properties. Covers enabling/disabling the warning, setting the warning notice period in seconds, and choosing the logout page shown after expiry. Also lists CDEJ text customizations for the modal title, pre-expiry message, post-expiry message, and Continue/Quit button labels. Notes that CDEJ settings apply globally, and a specific application’s timeout-warning configuration overrides both application and CDEJ properties.

children.title: Customizing the session timeout warning for a specific application
children.summary: Explains how to customize the session timeout warning per application by adding the optional `timeout-warning` element in an app’s `.app` configuration file. This overrides both system-level settings and defaults. Lists configurable attributes: dialog title, pre-expiry and post-expiry messages, Quit/Continue button text, warning lead time in seconds, and modal width/height. Notes that long messages may automatically show a scroll bar via CSS, which cannot be disabled by property.

children.title: Configuring a customized logon page
children.summary: Explains how to configure a customized logon page so users redirected after session timeout see a session-expiry message. Covers both the custom logon JSP approach (import `curam.util.client.jsp.JspUtil` and call `JspUtil.printSessionExpiredMessage(pageContext)`) and the custom logon renderer approach, which begins by wrapping the expiry message in a `div` with a custom ID. Also notes that the logon page is set via configuration properties for both Cúram and Universal Access.

Explains browser session timeout warnings and how to configure them globally, per application, or in Universal Access. Covers default modal text, width/height, and buffering settings from ApplicationConfiguration.properties and CDEJResources.properties, plus internal vs external property names, logout page selection, and application-level timeout-warning overrides. Also describes how to customize the warning in a specific app’s .app file and how to insert a session-expiry message into a custom logon JSP or renderer page.

children.title: 5.5 Tab session limitations
children.summary: Tab session data supports only about 70–80 tabs by total size. After this limit, tab state is kept only in the web tier and not written to the database; browser refresh restoration still works, but logout may restore only the state from when the limit was first exceeded. Closing tabs reduces data size and resumes database writes. The section also notes browser session sharing issues: multiple tabs/instances using the same session can interfere, and users must log out before switching users or mixing internal and external applications in the same browser.

children.title: 5.6 Browser Specific Session Management
children.summary: Browser version and shared browser sessions can affect Cúram session behavior. If two browser instances share one session, they show the same open tabs and can interfere with persisted data, similar to two users sharing credentials. Logging in as a different user in a new tab can cause the original tab’s view to switch after refresh, and new tabs may persist under the wrong user. Users must log out before switching users, and internal/external applications cannot be used concurrently in the same browser.

Cúram browser session management: sessions start on login and end on logout, browser shutdown, or inactivity timeout (default 30 minutes). Covers per-section open-tab limits (default 15, configurable), tab restoration on refresh vs new login, intermittent persistence of tab state to the database, and limits (~70–80 tabs total) that stop database writes. Also explains session timeout warning configuration globally, per app, and in Universal Access, plus custom logon-page expiry messages. Notes browser/session sharing risks and that internal and external apps can’t share one browser session.

children.title: 6 Browser management
6.1 Configuring browser Back, Refresh, and Close button behavior
children.summary: Explains how to configure Cúram browser Back, Refresh, and Close button behavior via system properties for the caseworker/internal and Universal Access/external UIs. By default, these buttons show browser-specific confirmation dialogs to prevent data loss, but the warnings can be enabled or disabled per application. Notes important browser quirks: Chrome/Edge add a “don’t show again” checkbox, Safari can disable the feature after Leave, tablets don’t show the dialog, and some dialogs may not appear if the page hasn’t been interacted with.

children.title: 6.2 Optimal browser support
Optimal browser support configuration
children.summary: Explains how to configure optimal browser support in ApplicationConfiguration.properties. Covers enabling/disabling browser detection, setting the days until the next browser check, and defining minimum/maximum supported browser versions for Chrome and Firefox. Notes default values (detection off, next check 60 days, version limits default to 0), valid value types, and that invalid settings fall back to defaults with server-log exceptions when client-side tracing is enabled.

children.title: Optimal browser message configuration
children.summary: Explains how to configure the “optimal browser” banner in ApplicationConfiguration.properties. Covers browser version thresholds elsewhere on the page and, here, the message text and accessibility labels: optimal.browser.msg.description, optimal.browser.msg.text (supports optional hyperlink markup {0.link:...}{0.end}), optimal.browser.msg.info, optimal.browser.dismiss, and optimal.browser.warning. Notes defaults exist for several fields and that malformed link/markup prevents expected rendering.

Describes configurable optimal browser support in `ApplicationConfiguration.properties`. Covers enabling/disabling browser detection, days until the next browser check (default 60, integer), and supported version thresholds for Chrome/Firefox (plus Safari/Edge), with invalid values falling back to 0 and logging exceptions when client-side tracing is enabled. Also documents optimal browser banner text/accessibility settings: description, main message with optional `{0.link:...}{0.end}` hyperlink markup, rendering info text, dismiss tooltip, and warning label.

Covers browser management for Cúram: configuring Back/Refresh/Close confirmation warnings for internal (caseworker) and external (Universal Access) apps via system properties, with browser-specific quirks and limits. Also documents optimal browser support settings in ApplicationConfiguration.properties: enabling detection, next-check interval, supported min/max versions for Chrome/Firefox/Safari/Edge, and the banner’s accessible message text, link markup, info, dismiss tooltip, and warning label.

children.title: 7 Autorecovery
7.1 Technical overview
children.summary: This section explains how Autorecovery works technically alongside session management to restore users to the same modal and previously entered data after interruptions such as tab refresh, tab close/reopen, or session timeout after login. It notes that supported field values are cached as users enter or leave them, recovery is global across the application/portals, and it must be enabled in system administration. It also describes creation of an autorecovery record and storage from client cache to the database.

children.title: 7.2 Areas supported by autorecovery
children.summary: This section lists where autorecovery applies in Cúram. It supports UIM modal dialogs only, including standard/dynamic/generated modals, wizards, MDT portals, and the main application for all user types. It does not support modals launched from other modals, or modals with no supported fields. It also begins listing supported form elements, including text input fields.

children.title: 7.3 Supported scenarios
children.summary: Autorecovery handles supported interruptions such as session timeouts, browser tab refreshes, and tabs/windows being closed and reopened. If the session has timed out, the user can log back in with any supported browser. If the tab or window is accidentally closed while the session is still active, reopening it restores the data.

children.title: 7.4 Enabling autorecovery
children.summary: Explains how to enable and use autorecovery. Covers configuration properties for turning autorecovery on, CuramFormsAPI dependency, throttling, and tab session update threshold, plus guidance for production rollout/restart, disabling, and data retention. Notes that autorecovery stores in-process data in browser cache and can be lost if cache is cleared or upgrades occur without saving. Also details supported recovery behavior across multiple tabs/windows and important limits: no support with lost connectivity before timeout, VPN interruptions, multiple browsers/incognito, shared logins, or concurrent devices.

Autorecovery restores in-progress modal data after tab refresh/close/reopen or session timeout, working with session management to return users to the same modal and last entered values. It is global when enabled in system administration, stores supported field changes in client cache then database on timeout, and keeps one record per user. Supported areas are UIM modals, wizards, MDT portals, and the main app; unsupported are nested modals and many field types. The section also covers throttling, supported interruption scenarios, limitations, and production enablement/configuration.

children.title: 8 Domain-specific controls
8.1 Dates
Three Field Date Selector
children.summary: Describes the THREE_FIELD_DATE domain, an alternate date selector widget for server access bean fields. Unlike the standard pop-up calendar, it renders three drop-downs for month, day, and year. The order and month labels follow the dateformat setting in ApplicationConfiguration.properties. Day values run 1–31, with infrastructure validation preventing invalid dates (for example, February 31). Useful if you need the details of this date input control and its dependence on application date-format configuration.

Dates map to the SVR_DATE domain and use a JavaScript-based date selector with an adjacent pop-up calendar for entering date/date-time values. The selector can be styled via CSS, and its validation format is configurable through curam.validation.calendar.dateFormat, otherwise it defaults to ApplicationConfiguration.properties. The section also introduces THREE_FIELD_DATE, an alternate widget using three drop-downs for month/day/year, with order and month labels driven by the application dateformat setting and infrastructure validation preventing invalid dates.

children.title: 8.2 Date-Times
Representing Time-Only Values
children.summary: Cúram has no native base type for time-only values. Instead, the provided CURAM_TIME domain is used by the client infrastructure to render a time-only widget and handle parsing/formatting. Under the hood, it still uses the same representation as SVR_DATETIME, so server-side code must completely ignore the date portion when processing time-only fields.

children.title: Customizing the Time Format
