children.summary: Explains how CURAM_TIME fields use an editable combo box with 30-minute selectable values, direct entry, and application-wide formatting from ApplicationConfiguration.properties, including AM/PM for 12-hour display. Notes default behavior for blank values and 00:00 inputs, then states that the time format can be customized by changing the timeformat and timeseparator properties. Points to the application configuration properties section for more details.

Date-times use the SVR_DATETIME domain and display a date selector plus time entry field; the picker requires JavaScript. The date-time validation format is configurable in CDEJResources.properties via curam.validation.calendar.dateTimeFormat, defaulting to HH mm ss if unset. Cúram also provides CURAM_TIME for time-only input: it renders a time-only widget but still stores SVR_DATETIME, so server code must ignore the date portion.

children.title: 8.3 Frequency Pattern Selector
children.summary: This section explains the FREQUENCY_PATTERN domain’s frequency pattern selector pop-up. It lets users choose patterns such as daily, weekly, monthly, bi-monthly, or yearly via a non-editable HTML text field with an adjacent pop-up icon. The selector is JavaScript-based, so browser JavaScript must be enabled. Its appearance can be customized through a dedicated CSS file. It also notes that selected pattern text varies in length, which may affect wrapping and layout in UIM pages.

children.title: 8.4 Selection lists
Drop-down, scrollable and check-boxed list types
children.summary: Explains selection list presentation options: default drop-down versus scrollable lists (set FIELD HEIGHT > 1), and check-boxed lists (CONTROL="CHECKBOXED_LIST") as an alternate display of scrollable lists. Covers enabling multiple selection only for scrollable lists via curam-config.xml MULTIPLE_SELECT entries, with selected values passed as tab-delimited strings and requiring string-based target/source properties. Also notes code-table-backed drop-downs show only enabled entries, sorted by description unless sort order is overridden.

children.title: Enabling multiple selection in lists
children.summary: Explains how to enable multiple selection for selection lists in Cúram. Multiple selection is supported only for scrollable lists, not drop-down lists, and is activated in curam-config.xml via a MULTIPLE_SELECT section with DOMAIN entries set to MULTIPLE="true". A FIELD bound to one of those domains allows multi-select; submitted values are returned as a tab-delimited string, so the target property must use a string domain. Initial values are also expected as a tab-separated string matching HIDDEN_PROPERTY values.

children.title: Populated from a code table
children.summary: Describes how a FIELD mapped to a code-table domain populates a drop-down list: only enabled code-table entries are shown, and they are sorted alphabetically by code description by default. Notes that this default ordering can be overridden by setting each entry’s sort order.

children.title: Populated from Server Interface Properties
children.summary: Explains how to populate a selection list from server interface properties using an INITIAL connection. Covers insert-page and modify-page examples, with PROPERTY supplying visible values and HIDDEN_PROPERTY mapping the selected visible item to a hidden target property. On insert pages the first item is selected by default; on modify pages a SOURCE connection sets the initial selection. Also notes that if HIDDEN_PROPERTY is omitted, the visible value can be mapped directly to the target property.

children.title: Transfer List widget
children.summary: Describes the Transfer List widget for multiple selection: two side-by-side HTML select controls, with available items on the left, selected items on the right, and four buttons to move items individually or all at once. Explains configuration via global TRANSFER_LISTS_MODE=true in curam-config.xml or per-field CONTROL="TRANSFER_LIST" on UIM FIELD elements. Notes it uses the same data and multiple-selection setup as a regular list.

Section 8.4 covers HTML selection lists in Cúram: blank entries for non-mandatory fields; drop-down vs scrollable lists via FIELD HEIGHT; checkboxed lists via CONTROL="CHECKBOXED_LIST"; and multiple selection only for scrollable lists through curam-config.xml MULTIPLE_SELECT, returning tab-delimited strings and requiring string domains. It also explains code-table-backed lists (enabled entries only, default alphabetical sorting unless overridden) and populating lists from server interface properties using INITIAL, SOURCE, TARGET, and optional HIDDEN_PROPERTY mappings.

children.title: 8.5 User Preferences Editor
children.summary: The User Preferences Editor lets users view and edit application preference values from the taskbar preferences button. It opens a popup listing all visible preferences, with editable ones shown as text fields, radio buttons, or drop-downs depending on type. Users save changes with Submit Changes, which take effect immediately, or restore original values with Reset to Default.

children.title: 8.6 Rules Trees
Default Rules View
children.summary: Describes the RULES_DEFINITION domain’s rules tree display options via the FIELD CONTROL attribute. The default rules view (CONTROL=DEFAULT) shows the full expanded tree in standard HTML and may be verbose. It also covers SUMMARY view (hides failure/success details but shows summary items), FAILURE view (similar layout, focused on failed rule items), and DYNAMIC view (compressed, expanding/contracting decision tree). Notes that the summary flag has no effect in the default view, while highlight-on-failure colors failed rules differently.

children.title: Summary Rules View
children.summary: Explains RULES_DEFINITION rules-tree display options via the FIELD CONTROL attribute. The summary view (CONTROL=SUMMARY) shows the tree without failure/success details, while still displaying all items marked as summary items. It contrasts this with DEFAULT, FAILURE, and DYNAMIC views, and notes that the summary-flag has no effect here, while highlight-on-failure changes failed-item color. Useful for understanding how rules output can be formatted and filtered in the rules editor.

children.title: Failed Rules View
children.summary: Explains the Failed Rules View for RULES_DEFINITION rules trees. Set FIELD CONTROL to FAILURE to show a summary-style tree that displays only items of type rule (not objectives or rule groups). Notes that the summary flag does not affect this view, while highlight-on-failure colors failed rules differently. The view is configurable like the dynamic rules view.

children.title: Dynamic Rules View
Behavior of Summary and Highlight-On-Failure Indicator
children.summary: This section explains how summary items and highlight-on-failure behave in the rules tree view. The highlight-on-failure indicator has no effect in this view. Failed items marked as summaries are shown as separate trees only if no ancestor group in their parent path is also a failed summary item. It also continues the configuration discussion for decision ID sourcing in widget links, including sourcing from a page parameter or from a bean field, and notes a validation error if conflicting source attributes are set.

Describes the Dynamic Rules View for RULES_DEFINITION widgets. When CONTROL is DYNAMIC, the decision is shown as an interactive, compressed tree of failed summary items rather than a full static tree. Covers configuration via RulesDecisionConfig.xml, including DEFAULT, HYPERLINK-TEXT, OPEN-NODE-PARAM, and decision ID propagation from either a page parameter or bean field, with validation if both source forms are set. Notes styling/localization files and that highlight-on-failure has no effect; failed summary items appear separately only when no ancestor summary item also failed.

children.title: Dynamic Full Tree Rules View
children.summary: Describes the DYNAMIC_FULL_TREE control view for Rules Engine decisions. It is similar to the Dynamic Rules View but displays the entire rule set with no filtering of rule groups, and the tree remains interactive. Configuration is done in RulesDecisionConfig.xml. The section contrasts it with the dynamic rules view, which filters results based on success/failure and summary flags, and notes the full-tree view can be harder for unfamiliar users to interpret.

children.title: Rules Editor
children.summary: Describes the RULES_DEFINITION domain’s Rules Editor widget. It has a default HTML view, or an SVG dynamic view when FIELD CONTROL is DYNAMIC. The widget is configured via the CONFIG attribute, which must match a CONFIG ID in RulesEditorConfig.xml. Explains that the XML is merged at build time and shows sample configurations defining DEFAULT, hyperlink behavior, node icons, edit pages, and decision ID/source-target mappings for different rule types.

Covers RULES_DEFINITION rules trees and editor behavior. Explains FIELD CONTROL options: DEFAULT expanded HTML tree, SUMMARY hides failure/success details but shows summary items, FAILURE shows only rule-type summary items, DYNAMIC shows compressed interactive trees of failed summary items, and DYNAMIC_FULL_TREE shows the full interactive rule set. Describes summary/highlight-on-failure effects, XML configuration via RulesDecisionConfig.xml/RulesEditorConfig.xml, hyperlink and edit-page mapping, open-node and decision-ID parameters, and a validation rule for decision ID sources.

children.title: 8.7 Meeting View
Meeting View XML format
children.summary: Defines the Meeting View XML format used by the meeting view control, with configuration stored in MeetingViewConfig.xml. Describes the required <SCHEDULE> structure, including MODE (Single/Multiple), TYPE, READ_ONLY, DATE, USER elements, and BUSY time ranges. Explains attribute formats for dates, durations, and time slots. Also shows the companion <SCHEDULE_CONFIG> format with CONFIG settings such as INTERVAL, START/END times, and linked actions for user home, new event, and multi-select pages.

The meeting view control displays scheduling information as a chart for the USER_DAILY_SCHEDULE domain. It supports single-select mode, where available time slots are hyperlinks for choosing a meeting start time, and multiple-select mode, where users are selected via checkboxes and time slots are display-only. The section defines the required XML structure in MeetingViewConfig.xml: <SCHEDULE> with MODE, TYPE, READ_ONLY, DATE, USER, and BUSY elements, plus <SCHEDULE_CONFIG> with per-type settings such as INTERVAL, START/END times, and linked pages for user home, new event, and multi-select actions.

children.title: 8.8 Charts
Chart appearance
children.summary: This section explains chart appearance for bar and column charts in Cúram. It describes how bar charts show stacked colored bars with axes, captions, tooltips, and hyperlinkable bars/captions. It notes that chart scale auto-fits the largest stack but can be overridden, and that long captions wrap or truncate with a tooltip and enlarge option. Column charts use the same configuration, but with vertical stacks and swapped axes, and can include a legend.

children.title: Chart configuration
Customizing chart colors in system administration
children.summary: Explains how to customize chart colors in Cúram system administration for Assessment Delivery Results, Details, Tracking, and Factor Ratings Line charts. Lists the default RGB values and the relevant application properties: curam.assessmentplanning.graphRGBColors and curam.outcomeplanning.factorGraphRGBColor. Describes the admin steps to change values via Property Administration, save, and publish. Also notes related chart text/link configuration details, including caption/link behavior and localization via BarChart.properties.

children.title: Customizing colors on horizontal and vertical bar charts
children.summary: Explains how to customize chart colors in Cúram. For horizontal and vertical bar charts, edit `charts.xsl` and change the `<ibm:colors>` values, then run a client build; the default palette was chosen for contrast ratio compliance. Also covers changing the Participation Summary chart’s Scheduled Hours and Actual Hours background/hover colors by editing `ParticipationSummaryResultRenderer.java` and updating the JSON color string, followed by a client build.

children.title: Customizing colors on the Participation Summary chart
children.summary: Explains how to customize chart colors in Cúram. For horizontal/vertical bar charts, edit `charts.xsl` and update the `<ibm:colors>` sequence, then run a client build; note the default palette was chosen to satisfy contrast guidelines. For the Participation Summary chart, modify `ParticipationSummaryResultRenderer.java` to change the Scheduled Hours and Actual Hours background/hover colors (`#6929c4` and `#1192e8` by default), then rebuild.

Explains chart configuration via CONFIG entries in ChartConfig.xml/BarChartConfig.xml for bar, column, line, and pie charts: orientation/type, axis labels, captions, legends, hyperlinks, and localized text via BarChart.properties. Also describes customizing chart colors in system admin for assessment and factor rating charts through application properties and publish, plus editing charts.xsl for horizontal/vertical bar chart colors and ParticipationSummaryResultRenderer.java for Participation Summary colors, followed by a client build.

children.title: Chart data formats
children.summary: Charts are populated from server XML. The root CHART contains any number of UNIT groups, each with one CAPTION and one or more BLOCK elements. CAPTION can show a date/range via START_DATE and END_DATE, or text resolved from a code table, BarChart.properties, or literal text. BLOCK defines a chart bar/column and requires TYPE and LENGTH; ID is a unique hyperlink parameter and DUE_DATE is an optional hyperlink parameter. BLOCK may carry extra attributes, but reserved names have specific meanings.

This section explains Cúram chart widgets: bar and column chart appearance, scaling, tooltips, hyperlinks, long caption wrapping/truncation, and optional legends. It details chart configuration in ChartConfig.xml/BarChartConfig.xml via CONFIG, LINK, LEGEND, and CAPTION_LINK settings, including axis labels, captions, localization, and color customization (application properties, XSL, Java renderer). It also defines the XML chart data format: CHART/UNIT/CAPTION/BLOCK structure, caption date/text rules, and BLOCK attributes such as TYPE, LENGTH, ID, and DUE_DATE for links.

children.title: 8.9 Heatmap Widget
Configuration
children.summary: Defines Heatmap widget configuration in `HeatmapConfig.xml` for domain-specific controls. Explains that heatmaps show item importance by color from red to blue, require XML `<HEATMAP>` data with multiple `<REGION>` elements, and may need empty regions for proper rendering. Shows configuration structure with `<CONFIG>` entries such as `ID`, `NUM_COLS`, `NUM_ROWS`, legend settings, and `<ITEM_LINK>`/`<PARAM>` mappings. Includes the `NUM_COLS` attribute purpose: number of items per row.

Describes the Heatmap widget, a domain-specific control that displays item importance as a red-to-blue grid. Explains that it is enabled via the XML_HEATMAP domain on a FIELD and requires server XML in a <HEATMAP> structure with at least two <REGION> elements, where regions represent heat levels and may need empty placeholders for correct rendering. Also covers HeatmapConfig.xml configuration, including <CONFIG> settings like ID, NUM_COLS, NUM_ROWS, legend options, and ITEM_LINK/PARAM mappings.

children.title: 8.10 Workflow
Workflow XML Formats
children.summary: Defines the XML formats used by workflow widgets. It explains the workflow schema (workflow.xsd), the WORKFLOW root with NODE and EDGE elements, and the main attributes for activities and transitions, including execution state, task status, IDs, coordinates, and hyperlink behavior. It also covers workflow configuration via WorkflowConfig.xml (workflow-config.xsd), with CONFIG and ICON elements, and key settings such as pages, code tables, start/end process types, readonly mode, highlighting, and notification icons.

Defines the Workflow widget and its XML/configuration. Workflow data comes from server XML matching workflow.xsd, with WORKFLOW roots containing NODE activities and EDGE transitions. Nodes carry IDs, coordinates, labels, execution/task status, split/join types, dates, and hyperlinks; edges define from/to nodes, execution/followed state, arrow direction, ordering, and conditions. WorkflowConfig.xml (workflow-config.xsd) supplies per-widget settings and icons, including details/notification pages, start/end process types, code tables, readonly mode, highlighting, and instance-data display.

children.title: 8.11 Evidence view
Evidence view configuration
children.summary: Explains how to configure the Evidence view, which has display and comparison modes. Use DisplayEvidence.properties for EVIDENCE_XML and ComparedEvidence.properties for EVIDENCE_XML_COMPARE, placed under <CLIENT_DIR>\components\core. The properties define table captions, column headers, column visibility, links for evidence item/group columns, and text for empty values and item-name substitutions. Visibility values must be true/false, not localizable. Includes sample property settings for both modes, including modified/new/removed evidence tables and links to UIM pages.

children.title: Evidence view XML data formats
children.summary: Defines the XML structure required by Evidence View for Evidence Comparison mode. Describes a top-level `<EVIDENCE_COMPARE>` element containing `<EVIDENCE>` blocks with types like `MODIFIED`, `NEW`, and `REMOVED`, each containing `<GROUP>` elements and `<EVIDENCE_ITEM>` entries. Shows how items use attributes such as `ID`, `DESCRIPTION`, `VALUE`, `OLDVAL`, and `DOMAIN` to represent changed, added, or deleted evidence data.

Explains the Evidence view for domain-specific controls. It has two modes: Evidence display mode for `EVIDENCE_XML`, showing a table of evidence item name, group, and formatted value; and Evidence comparison mode for `EVIDENCE_XML_COMPARE`, showing modified, new, and removed evidence in separate tables, with modified items including old and new values. Describes configuration via `DisplayEvidence.properties` and `ComparedEvidence.properties` in `<CLIENT_DIR>\components\core`, including captions, headers, visibility flags, links, and empty-value text. Also gives the required comparison XML structure.

children.title: 8.12 Calendar
children.summary: Explains the Calendar domain-specific control for UIM pages using CALENDAR_XML_STRING. Defines the server XML schema for calendar data, including EVENT and SINGLE_DAY_EVENT elements, required/optional attributes, and view types (day, week, month via CVT1–CVT3). Describes CalendarConfig.xml for per-calendar configuration: MONTH_CELL_HEIGHT, SHOW_REPEAT_EVENT_TEXT, CALENDAR TYPE mapping, DESCRIPTION_LOCATION links, DAY_VIEW_TIME_FORMAT, and EVENT_TYPES icon mapping. Includes hyperlink parameters passed to event description pages and notes related XSD locations.

children.title: 8.13 Payment Statement view
children.summary: Explains the Payment Statement view, used to display under- or over-payments in Cúram. It supports both benefit and liability statements, using BENEFIT_REASSESSMENT_RESULT_TEXT and LIABILITY_REASSESSMENT_RESULT_TEXT domains with tab-delimited string data. Describes the PaymentStatement.properties configuration for row links to detail pages (PageID, ParameterName, Label, Image) and lists externalized UI text labels such as Action, Period, Actual, Reassessed, Difference, and various totals.

children.title: 8.14 Batch Function View
children.summary: Batch Function View describes a UI generated from the PARAM_TAB_LIST domain for entering parameters and submitting a batch program. Field labels are supplied as one tab-delimited string. This section is brief and primarily introduces the batch function input view.

children.title: 8.15 Addresses
children.summary: Explains the ADDRESS_DATA domain type for capturing/displaying addresses as a single stored string with multiple UI fields. Describes how address layouts are defined in curam-config.xml using ADDRESS_CONFIG, LOCALE_MAPPING, ADDRESS_FORMAT, and ADDRESS_ELEMENT. Covers locale-specific formats (US, UK, DE, CA, KR, JP, TW, CN), default country code behavior, optional description text, code-table dropdowns, and mandatory/conditional mandatory address fields. Useful for understanding configurable address parsing, display, and localization.

children.title: 8.16 Schedule view
children.summary: Explains the schedule view for SCHEDULE_DATA domains. It renders a grid from 8 am to 8 pm in half-hour cells, with each row showing a person’s full name and availability; clicking a free cell links to a page for adding schedule events. Describes required UIM setup: a FIELD followed by a LINK with PAGE_ID, and ordered CONNECTs for date, full user name, and user name/ID. Also notes the data format as tab-separated user name plus 32-bit schedule field.

children.title: 8.17 Radio button group
children.summary: This section explains how to present code table values as a radio button group. Each radio button corresponds to a code table item, and fields using this display must map to the SHORT_CODETABLE_CODE domain or a domain that directly inherits from it.

children.title: 8.18 Pop-up pages
Configure a pop-up page
children.summary: Explains how to configure pop-up pages in curam-config.xml using POPUP_PAGES and POPUP_PAGE. Covers root DISPLAY_IMAGES setting, optional clear-text image, and how each pop-up is tied to a DOMAIN or CT_CODE. Documents attributes for search/create pop-up pages, control type and edit behavior, insertion mode, dialog size, launcher images/hover/high-contrast variants, and text labels with localization property overrides. Useful for defining search/create pop-ups for target fields and controlling their UI presentation.

children.title: Create a pop-up page
children.summary: Explains how to create Cúram pop-up pages in UIM. Covers two patterns: a pop-up that returns an existing selected item from search results, and a pop-up that creates a new item and returns its generated ID. Notes required settings such as PAGE_ID matching curam-config.xml, POPUP_PAGE="true", using THIS for redisplay on submit, DISMISS to close without values, and SUBMIT_AND_DISMISS for create-and-return flows. Also explains that pop-ups must return both value and description via PAGE target CONNECTs.

children.title: Using the pop-up page
children.summary: Explains how to open pop-up pages with standard UIM FIELD elements. If a field has a target connection based on a domain in curam-config.xml, it renders as a pop-up link instead of a text field. Shows basic insert-page and modify-page examples using TARGET, SOURCE, and INITIAL connections to display a name while submitting an ID. Also covers passing page parameters via a LINK element, including mixing page and server properties, and notes that LINK use forbids PAGE_ID and TEXT source connections.

children.title: Configure a multiple pop-up page
children.summary: Explains how to configure and use a multiple pop-up page. The config is defined in `curam-config.xml` via `MULTIPLE_POPUP_DOMAINS`, with `CLEAR_TEXT_IMAGE` and one or more `MULTIPLE_POPUP_DOMAIN` entries specifying the domain, icon image, and label. It notes that the pop-up type is chosen from a code-table, linked to the pop-up page by `POPUP_PAGE/CT_CODE`. Also shows the required UIM pattern: a `CONTAINER` with exactly two `FIELD`s, one for the code-table dropdown and one for the returned value.

children.title: Using a multiple pop-up page
children.summary: Explains how to configure and use a multiple pop-up page in curam-config.xml. Defines MULTIPLE_POPUP_DOMAINS, with CLEAR_TEXT_IMAGE for the clear button and MULTIPLE_POPUP_DOMAIN entries mapping a DOMAIN to popup icon LABEL and IMAGE. A dropdown selects the popup type via a code table, linked to the popup page through POPUP_PAGE/CT_CODE. Also shows the required UIM structure: one CONTAINER with exactly two FIELD elements, for the popup-type dropdown and the returned value.

Explains Cúram pop-up pages and multiple pop-up pages. Covers configuring pop-ups in `curam-config.xml` via `POPUP_PAGES`/`MULTIPLE_POPUP_DOMAINS`, including domain/CT code mapping, launcher/clear images, labels, dialog size, control type/edit mode, and localization overrides. Shows how to build UIM pop-up pages that return `value` and `description`, using `DISMISS` or `SUBMIT_AND_DISMISS`, and how standard fields open them via target connections. Multiple pop-ups add a code-table dropdown and require a container with exactly two fields.

children.title: 8.19 Agenda Player
Agenda Player screen structure
children.summary: Describes the Agenda Player wizard-style screen layout and use in Cúram. It explains that the player guides users through an agenda or scenario, is supported only in a modal dialog, and may have three or four regions: a top header with title/progress bar, an optional left navigation panel with agenda pages/sections and summary link, bottom navigation buttons (Back/Next/Finish/Cancel), and a main content area for client pages.

children.title: Navigation modes
children.summary: Describes Agenda Player navigation modes and when to use them: basic (strictly sequential, only standard buttons), incremental (default; links to already visited pages), and full (all pages linked, non-sequential, with possible loss of sequential features like progress bar or dynamic parameters). Notes that side pages opened via hyperlinks disable Player navigation until return. Also covers navigator-less/Claimant view, which hides the navigation panel, ignores mode settings, and adds a Finish button to jump to the summary page when present.

children.title: Navigator-less view
children.summary: Describes Agenda Player navigation modes and the navigator-less (Claimant) view. Three modes exist: basic (sequential only), incremental (links to visited pages), and full (all pages as links, non-sequential, with possible limitations like no progress bar). In Claimant view, the navigation panel is removed and only standard buttons work, so the mode setting is ignored. A Finish button appears automatically when a summary page exists, letting users jump directly to it.

children.title: Agenda Player configuration
children.summary: Explains how to configure and customize the Agenda Player in Cúram. It describes editing AgendaConfig.xml in the components directory and defines PLAYER attributes: ID, TITLE, MODE (basic, incremental default, or full), NAVIGATOR-HIDDEN, and CONFIRM-QUIT. It also covers localization/customization of player title, progress bar text, button labels, quit confirmation text, and frame descriptions via CDEJResources.properties and AgendaPlayer.properties.

children.title: Agenda Player customization
children.summary: Explains how to customize and localize the Agenda Player via `AgendaConfig.xml` and component resource files. Covers `PLAYER` configuration attributes (`ID`, `TITLE`, `MODE`, `NAVIGATOR-HIDDEN`, `CONFIRM-QUIT`) and their effects on navigation mode, hidden display in Claimant View, and quit confirmation. Also notes that customizable text includes the player title, progress bar text, button labels, quit dialog text, and frame descriptions, with values stored in `CDEJResources.properties` and `AgendaPlayer.properties` under the component directory.

children.title: Agenda Player data
children.summary: Explains Agenda Player configuration and agenda XML structure. Covers customizing progress bar text, control button labels, frame titles, and quit dialog via properties files. Describes required UIM pages: navigation page (SPLIT_WINDOW with AGENDA_XML field), optional summary page with WIZARD_SUMMARY, and exit page. Shows agenda XML example and page attributes such as id, description, status, initial, disableback/forward, submitonnext, and close-on-submit. Notes page order, dynamic parameters, exit behavior, summary-based quit, and status-to-icon mapping.

Describes Cúram’s Agenda Player wizard control for guided, modal-only navigation through agenda/scenario screens. Covers screen layout (header, optional navigation panel, buttons, content), navigation modes (basic, incremental default, full), and navigator-less/Claimant view with hidden navigation and automatic Finish to summary. Explains configuration via AgendaConfig.xml, customizable titles/buttons/text, and agenda data/UIM requirements: navigation page, optional summary page, exit page, page attributes, dynamic parameters, submit-on-next behavior, and status-to-icon mapping.

children.title: 8.20 LOCALIZED_MESSAGE Domain
children.summary: Explains the LOCALIZED_MESSAGE domain, used to display server message catalog entries on a client screen. It is string-based and requires a specific formatted message string. The Server Development Environment can format catalog entries for client return. After formatting on the server, the value must be assigned to a field based on this domain and sent to the client, where it is rendered according to the current locale with placeholders populated by values.

Covers Cúram domain-specific controls, where fields render specialized widgets instead of standard HTML controls. Includes date/date-time and time-only selectors, frequency pattern pop-up, selection lists (blank/default, scrollable, checkboxed, multiple select, code-table and server-populated), plus the Agenda Player wizard control with its navigation/summary/exit-page XML and status icons. Also introduces LOCALIZED_MESSAGE for catalog-formatted, locale-aware messages. Useful if you need control/domain mappings, XML/configuration details, or widget behavior.

children.title: 9 Custom data conversion and sorting
9.1 Data conversion and sorting operations
children.summary: Explains Cúram web application data conversion/sorting lifecycle and customization points. Covers operations on presentation-layer values: format Java objects to strings for XHTML; pre-parse to apply UML domain options like trimming, whitespace compression, and uppercasing; parse strings back to Java objects; pre-validate for size/range checks; validate for custom rules like check digits; and compare for list sorting. Notes supported customization mechanisms only, with some unsupported methods mentioned for context.

children.title: 9.2 Data conversion lifecycle
children.summary: Describes the CDEJ data conversion lifecycle for a single property. It explains how data moves from application server to display and back, with domain-specific operations at each stage. Reading: fetch via business operation, optionally sort related values with compare, format to string, then insert into XHTML. Writing: user submits a string, pre-parse applies whitespace/uppercase rules, mandatory check runs, parse converts to native object, pre-validate enforces size/value/pattern constraints, validate applies arbitrary rules, then the value is sent back. Read-write combines both flows.

children.title: 9.3 The domain hierarchy and domain plug-ins
children.summary: Explains how Cúram represents domains at runtime as a forest of domain trees, with each node implementing `curam.util.common.domain.Domain`. Covers key methods (`getName`, `getParent`, `getRootDomain`, `getChildren`, `getPlugIn`) and the inheritance of plug-ins from ancestor domains when none is configured locally. Notes that domain plug-ins provide domain-specific conversion and comparison behavior, and introduces the supported plug-in types, starting with the “converter” plug-in for format/parse/validate operations.

children.title: 9.4 Domain plug-ins
Converter Plug-ins
children.summary: Converter Plug-ins describes the DomainConverter interface, the main extension point for custom data conversion. It covers format(), parse(), validate(), getDomainClass(), getGenericLocale(), formatGeneric(), and parseGeneric(). The section explains that format/parse handle normal object-string conversion, validate checks domain constraints without modifying data, and the generic methods are used for UML domain definition options and XML-based data. Default implementations of the generic methods are usually sufficient; most customizations are done by implementing or overriding converter methods.

children.title: Comparator Plug-ins
children.summary: Comparator plug-ins use the DomainComparator interface to control sort order. It extends both DomainPlugIn and java.util.Comparator, so implementations provide compare(Object, Object) to define ordering and usually rely on inherited equals. The section sits within custom data conversion and sorting, following notes on client domain conversion and before default value plug-ins. Open this section if you need details on implementing custom comparators for domain-based sorting in Cúram.

children.title: Default Value Plug-ins
children.summary: The DomainDefault interface defines fallback default values for domains when no default is otherwise available. This section introduces its purpose as part of custom data conversion and sorting plug-ins, alongside nearby converter and comparator plug-in interfaces. It notes that the main methods are documented in the Cúram Javadoc, but does not describe their behavior here. Use this section if you need the API contract for supplying domain defaults.

This section introduces domain plug-ins as Java classes implementing `DomainPlugIn`, with inherited domain/locale/name access and default instance management. It focuses on the main plug-in types: `DomainConverter` for `format`, `parse`, `validate`, plus generic conversion for UML domain options and XML data; `ClientDomainConverter` for `preParse` and `preValidate`; `DomainComparator` for sort order via `compare`; and `DomainDefault` for fallback values. Useful if you need the API contracts and customization points for domain data conversion and sorting.

children.title: 9.5 Domain plug-in configuration
children.summary: Explains how domain plug-ins are configured in XML using a `domains` root with one `domain` per domain and nested `plug-in` entries for converter, comparator, and default behavior. Shows inheritance across the domain hierarchy, with `INTERNAL_ID` overriding only the converter while inheriting other plug-ins from `SVR_INT64`. Notes the built-in options plug-in is not configured directly, and that `domains-config.xml` provides the base setup but must not be edited; custom files override or extend it via normal component merge rules.

children.title: 9.6 Provided domain plug-ins
Extending existing plug-ins
children.summary: Explains that custom domain behavior should usually be built by extending supplied Cúram plug-ins rather than writing from scratch. Notes that existing plug-ins can be overridden fully or partially, and that abstract base classes are provided: AbstractConverter, AbstractComparator, and AbstractDefault. These supply default behaviors such as number formatting, pre-parse/pre-validate logic, default-name resolution, and generic parse/format support. Also stresses that all extend AbstractDomainPlugIn, which handles locale, domain properties, and plug-in instance management, and recommends reuse for upgrade safety.

children.title: Converter plug-ins
children.summary: Describes built-in converter plug-ins in curam.util.client.domain.convert and how they handle formatting, parsing, pre-parse, and pre-validate operations. Lists provided plug-ins for common domains (dates, numbers, money, strings, localized messages, code tables, internal IDs, bare integer variants, blobs, booleans). Details locale-aware vs non-locale-specific behavior, time zones, zero-value handling, UTF-8 input, and validation support (size, value, pattern) per plug-in. Notes pre-parse/pre-validate are configured in UML domain options, not via plug-ins.

children.title: Comparator plug-ins
Localized (cultural-aware) string sorting
children.summary: Explains localized, culture-aware string sorting using the Unicode Collation Algorithm. Sorting depends on the user locale and the configurable `curam.collator.strength` static property, which requires a server restart to change. Summarizes five strength levels: PRIMARY (base letters), SECONDARY (accents), TERTIARY (case, default), QUATERNARY (ignores punctuation/minor differences, also for Japanese JIS X 4061), and IDENTICAL (code-point tie-breaker). Invalid values fall back to the default and log a warning; higher strength may hurt performance.

This section lists the built-in comparator plug-ins used to determine list sort order for Cúram domains, including numeric, date/time, boolean, blob, string, money, and code-table types. It explains default behavior for each, such as numeric and chronological sorting, and notes that SVR_STRING and CODETABLE_CODE use non-locale-aware lexicographic ordering by default. It also states that default plug-ins can be overridden with case-insensitive or locale-aware comparators, with the latter depending on Java JRE locale support and incurring some performance cost.

children.title: Default value plug-ins
children.summary: This section introduces default value plug-ins, which supply default values when no values are available. It notes that these plug-ins are provided for specific domains in the `curam.util.client.domain.defaults` package. The page also includes the tail of the prior topic on localized string sorting, explaining `curam.collator.strength` values, its static/restart requirement, fallback to default on invalid input, and possible performance impact.

Cúram provides built-in domain plug-ins for conversion, comparison, and default values, mainly in `curam.util.client.domain.*`. It recommends extending supplied or abstract base classes (`AbstractConverter`, `AbstractComparator`, `AbstractDefault`) rather than writing plug-ins from scratch, for upgrade safety and built-in instance/locale handling. Converter plug-ins cover formatting, parsing, pre-parse, and pre-validate rules for common domains. Comparator plug-ins define list sort order, including locale-aware options for strings and code tables. The section also covers collation strength settings and built-in default-value plug-ins.

children.title: 9.7 Error Reporting
Infrastructure errors
children.summary: Describes built-in infrastructure errors and advises retrying, restarting the web app, or contacting support if persistent, while keeping the error page source for diagnosis. Introduces exception handling for customizations, focusing on two exception types: DomainException and ConversionException (a subclass used by converter plug-ins and tied to specific fields for user-facing field labels/error icons). Explains that exceptions carry message IDs and parameters, with localization resolved later via message catalogs, and notes custom exception classes can integrate custom catalogs.

children.title: Exception classes
children.summary: Explains the exception types used by plug-in methods: DomainException and its subtype ConversionException. ConversionException is for converter plug-ins and can be tied to a specific field so UI can show field-level error labels/icons. The section also describes how client exceptions carry message IDs, substitution values, and causal exceptions rather than final text, with localization resolved later via message catalogs. It introduces custom exception classes as a way to integrate custom localized message catalogs into client error reporting.

children.title: Custom exception classes
children.summary: Explains how custom exception classes integrate with Cúram client error reporting. The section distinguishes DomainException and ConversionException, noting custom code usually throws ConversionException to support field-level error display and localized messages. It shows a formulaic pattern for a custom exception class extending ConversionException, defining constructors and a MessageLocalizer tied to a custom message catalog. It also describes numeric message IDs, placeholder substitution (%0s, %1s, etc.), and Java properties-file catalogs for localized messages.

Covers error reporting for custom data conversion. First notes handling built-in infrastructure errors by retrying, restarting, or contacting support, and saving the error page source for diagnosis. Then explains the two exception types used by plug-ins: DomainException and ConversionException, with ConversionException supporting field-level error display. Describes how exceptions carry message IDs, arguments, and causal exceptions, with localization resolved later via message catalogs. Shows how to implement custom ConversionException subclasses tied to a custom properties-file message catalog.

children.title: 9.8 Java object representations
children.summary: Root-domain values are represented by Java objects of a fixed class shared by all descendant domains, and that class cannot be changed. Custom data conversion and comparison logic operates on these strings and Java objects, so knowing the underlying Java type is essential when customizing behavior.

children.title: 9.9 Customization guidelines for data conversion and sorting
Custom formatting
children.summary: Explains how to create custom formatting for domain values by subclassing an existing converter plug-in and overriding format. Typical uses include padding values, adding currency symbols, removing grouping separators, or converting dates to another calendar. The example shows a USDollarConverter extending SvrMoneyConverter and prefixing the formatted value with “$”, with configuration via domain-config to register the converter for a domain such as US_DOLLAR_AMOUNT. Notes that inherited comparator/default-value plug-ins need not be reconfigured.

children.title: Custom parsing
children.summary: Explains when and how to implement custom parsing for converter plug-ins. Use it when users enter values in formats existing parsers don’t recognize or when preprocessing is needed before submission. Examples include stripping currency symbols, translating dates, padding strings, or custom numeric calculations. The section outlines the steps: identify the existing converter, subclass it, override parse, optionally call the superclass or write parsing from scratch, and configure the new plug-in for the relevant domains.

children.title: Custom validation
children.summary: Explains custom validation for converter plug-ins. Validation can be done via UML domain definition options, a custom converter’s validate method, or both. Prefer UML options when they cover the need; use a plug-in for additional or unsupported checks such as check digits or arbitrary value restrictions. To implement, subclass the existing converter plug-in, override validate, optionally call the superclass validation, then add custom checks and throw an exception on failure.

children.title: Custom sorting
children.summary: Explains custom domain sorting in Cúram via comparator plug-ins. Covers how list sorting works, that compare receives Java objects and can use locale, and basic steps: extend AbstractComparator, implement compare, configure per domain. Gives examples for numeric sorting of string IDs by zero-padding, sorting formatted/encoded values via the domain converter, and moving the zero date to the end. Notes comparator inheritance and a caution that comparators also affect pre-validation against domain min/max values.

children.title: Custom error reporting
children.summary: This section explains how to customize error reporting in Cúram converter plug-ins, especially for pre-validation pattern matching failures. It notes that default “not in a recognized format” messages may be unsuitable for obscure formats, and recommends either replacing pattern validation entirely or more simply intercepting and rethrowing exceptions with a clearer localized message. It applies mainly to parse and preValidate methods, and outlines the steps to subclass the converter plug-in, catch the relevant exception, verify its error number, and configure the new plug-in for the needed domains.

children.title: Custom default values
children.summary: Explains when and how to customize a default value plug-in for a domain. Usually the UML domain default is sufficient, but customization may be needed for assumed or dynamic defaults such as “current date.” The process is to identify an existing default value plug-in, subclass it, override getDisplayedDefault, return a value compatible with the root domain’s Java type, and configure the new plug-in for the relevant domains.

Guidelines for customizing Cúram data conversion and sorting plug-ins. Covers how to extend existing converter plug-ins for formatting, parsing, validation, and error reporting, and comparator plug-ins for custom sort order; also notes default value plug-ins briefly. Key patterns: subclass the existing plug-in, override the relevant method, optionally call super, then configure the plug-in per domain in domain-config and ensure runtime availability. Examples include dollar formatting/parsing, even-ID validation, numeric string sorting, zero-date ordering, and custom localized SSN error messages.

children.title: 9.10 Type checking and null checking
children.summary: Null and type checking are unnecessary in custom domain plug-in methods: the client infrastructure guarantees that checked values are never null and that conversion methods are only called with objects compatible with the class returned by getDomainClass. Therefore, custom code does not need to include error handling or reporting for these cases.

children.title: 9.11 Plug-in instance management
children.summary: Explains domain plug-in instance pooling in Cúram clients. Plug-ins keep state for domain and user locale, so the framework maintains pooled instances per domain and per locale to avoid concurrency issues and synchronization overhead. Plug-ins must not store custom state in fields and should use accessor methods like getDomain and getLocale. AbstractDomainPlugIn provides the standard pooling strategy. Also notes plug-ins must have a default constructor, though subclasses of provided plug-in base classes usually get one automatically.

children.title: 9.12 Naming conventions
children.summary: Use underscore-prefixed utility method names in custom domain plug-ins to avoid accidentally overriding inherited methods or future Cúram methods. Also avoid creating classes in packages that could conflict with Cúram package names; package names starting with “curam” are reserved, while “custom” is just an example, not a requirement.

children.title: 9.13 Generic parse operations
children.summary: Explains the generic parse operation in `DomainConverter.parseGeneric`, which parses non-locale-specific domain option values from the UML model, including max/min/default values and root-domain formats like ISO-8601 for `SVR_DATE`. It warns not to override default `parseGeneric` behavior unintentionally because `AbstractConverter.parseGeneric` delegates to `parse`. Custom converters may need separate implementations of `parse` and `parseGeneric` if their behaviors differ; provided concrete converters already separate them.

children.title: 9.14 Code tables
children.summary: Code-table domains are managed through the code-table administration interface, using the same plug-in mechanism as other domains but without supporting customization of code-table plug-ins. Because code-table formatting and parsing are asymmetric and can be fully handled administratively, no programmatic customization is needed. Formatting is done by changing code descriptions; parsing simply passes through code values. Pre-parsing, pre-validation, and validation are not significant here, and default codes plus custom sort orders are controlled entirely by the administration interface.

Explains Cúram’s custom data conversion/sorting framework for web UIs: the value lifecycle from server retrieval to XHTML display and back, including format, pre-parse, parse, pre-validate, validate, and compare. Describes domain hierarchy and plug-ins, their XML configuration and inheritance, provided converter/comparator/default plug-ins, error reporting, Java object root-domain types, and customization guidelines. Also covers null/type checking guarantees, plug-in pooling and state constraints, naming conventions, generic parse behavior, and why code-table domains are not programmatically customizable.

children.title: 10 Online help development
10.1 The online help system
children.summary: Explains the Cúram online help system for client pages. Help is defined in UIM-related properties files, with help entries placed immediately after the corresponding property definitions. The help content is generated during the client build target and rendered dynamically at runtime, so it does not require separate deployment. Covers the single-source approach, the table-style generation of field/action help, and integrated localization of both UI and help properties.

children.title: 10.2 Online help elements
children.summary: Explains how Cúram online help is defined and generated from client property files tied to UIM/VIM metadata. Help entries are externalized alongside UI properties, localized with the application, and generated dynamically at runtime as part of the client build. This section specifically covers online help elements: page descriptions, field/column help, and link/action help, with examples of naming patterns like `Help.PageDescription` and `*.Help`. Useful for understanding how to author help text for page controls and actions.

children.title: 10.3 Adding or updating help content
children.summary: Explains how to add or update online help content for a page: add a help property to the page’s UIM file and place the help text in the matching properties file. For updates, edit only the custom copy of the properties file under webclient/components/custom, then rebuild the client. Notes that help is generated dynamically at runtime. Includes special guidance for domain-specific controls, where help is added at the control-group level rather than individual locale-dependent fields, with an address example.

Explains Cúram’s context-sensitive online help for client pages. Help is authored in UIM-associated properties files, with help entries placed immediately after the related UI property and generated as part of the client build, so no separate deployment is needed. Covers single-source maintenance, integrated localization, and the generated table format for page descriptions, fields/columns, and links/actions. Also describes how to add or update help in custom property files and notes special handling for domain-specific controls such as addresses.

children.title: 11 Maintaining Dynamic UIM pages
11.1 Working in a development environment
children.summary: Explains how to load a dynamic UIM page into the resource store during development by adding two AppResource.dmx entries in the custom component: one for the .uim file and one for its .properties file. Includes an example for DUIMSample showing required fields such as resourceid, name, content path, versionNo, and category values RS_XML and RS_PROP. Useful for developers configuring build-time resource loading for dynamic UIM pages.

children.title: 11.2 Working in a running system
Search for dynamic UIM pages by category
children.summary: Explains how to view dynamic UIM pages in the Resource Store by category from the home Dynamic UIM administration screen. Users log into the admin application, open the Dynamic UIM menu, choose a category from the Category Search drop-down, and click Search to list pages in that category. The section also notes that dynamic UIM resources in AppResource.dmx are categorized with values like RS_XML for UIM files and RS_PROP for properties files, with the same category value used to group related resources.

children.title: Uploading a dynamic UIM page to the resource store
children.summary: Explains how to manage dynamic UIM pages in the resource store from the home administration screen. Covers adding a page with mandatory Page ID, locale, UIM file, and properties file; the Page ID must match the UIM file’s PAGE_ID attribute. Also describes editing existing pages by downloading the current ZIP, modifying the UIM/properties files, and re-uploading them, plus deleting a page via the list actions menu with confirmation.

children.title: Editing a dynamic UIM page in the resource store
children.summary: Explains how to manage dynamic UIM pages in the resource store from the home administration screen. Covers uploading a new page (Page ID must match the UIM file’s PAGE_ID, locale selection, and browsing for UIM and properties files), editing an existing page by Page ID (download current ZIP, extract and edit files, then re-upload the UIM and properties files), and deleting a page with confirmation.

children.title: Deleting a dynamic UIM File from the resource store
children.summary: Explains how to delete a dynamic UIM page from the resource store via the home dynamic UIM administration screen. From the list of pages, locate the target by Page ID, choose Delete from the list actions menu, then confirm by clicking Yes. Also includes nearby procedures for uploading and editing dynamic UIM pages, including required fields and download/edit/upload steps.

children.title: Validating a dynamic UIM file in the resource store
children.summary: Explains how to validate a dynamic UIM page in the resource store from the home dynamic UIM administration screen. To validate, find the page by Page ID and choose Validate from the list actions menu. A modal reports pass/fail; on failure, it shows the source error page and details are available in server logs. Also notes that changes to dynamic UIM files are not public until published to the resource store.

children.title: Publish dynamic UIM files
children.summary: Explains how to publish dynamic UIM files from the home dynamic UIM administration screen. Dynamic UIM changes are not public until published to the resource store; the user selects Publish and confirms. The section also notes nearby maintenance tasks: validating a dynamic UIM page by Page ID via the actions menu, with pass/fail shown in a modal and errors logged on the server, and that a search in the resource store can confirm removal of a dynamic UIM page.

Covers runtime maintenance of dynamic UIM pages in the Resource Store from the admin application’s Dynamic UIM screen. Includes how to search pages by category, add new pages (Page ID must match the UIM file’s PAGE_ID; locale, UIM, and properties files are required), edit existing pages by downloading and re-uploading files, delete pages with confirmation, validate pages with pass/fail feedback and server-log details, and publish changes so they become public. Also notes resource categorization in AppResource.dmx (RS_XML, RS_PROP, same category grouping).

Explains how to maintain dynamic UIM pages in the application resource store, distinguishing development-time and runtime approaches. In development, add two AppResource.dmx entries per page (UIM and properties) with categories RS_XML and RS_PROP, matching locale and content paths. In a running system, the admin “Dynamic UIM” screen lets users search by category, add pages (Page ID must match PAGE_ID), edit by downloading/reuploading, delete with confirmation, validate with pass/fail and server-log details, and publish changes to make them public.

children.title: 12 UI test automation
children.summary: Describes UI test automation using the data-testid attribute to reliably target application components. Lists supported components (checkboxes, date/time pickers, dropdowns, text inputs, modal buttons, etc.), naming formats, default prefixes, and how custom renderers generate IDs via context.getDataTestID() with configurable prefixes. Includes example plugin configurations for text, date, time, and checkbox renderers. Explains benefits over older selectors: more readable, less brittle, language-independent, and easier to maintain, with examples replacing generic IDs, title-based selectors, and long DOM paths.

children.title: 13 UIM reference
13.1 UIM document types
children.summary: Defines UIM document types in Cúram web client metadata. UIM is XML used to specify web application pages and is translated by the CDEJ into JSP; schemas support editor validation. Two document types are described: PAGE (.uim) defines a page, must match PAGE_ID, and be unique within a component; VIEW (.vim) defines reusable page fragments with flexible filenames but unique names within a component. Also notes UIM page structure and schema-imposed element ordering.

children.title: 13.2 UIM pages
children.summary: UIM pages are the main Cúram web UI elements, defined in .uim files, each representing a single page with titles, labels, buttons, links, and data content. The section notes that UIM pages must follow XML Schema ordering, though the JSP generator does not currently enforce it; schema-aware XML editors can help with validation and autocompletion. It also points readers to the web client overview for related concepts such as clusters, lists, action sets, action controls, containers, and fields.

children.title: 13.3 UIM views
children.summary: Explains UIM views, which are reusable fragments included in a PAGE via INCLUDE during translation. A view’s root element is VIEW, and included content must be valid in context and obey page element order; it cannot add conflicting elements like an existing PAGE_TITLE. Views cannot include other views and have no PAGE_ID, which comes from the including page. Also notes that only the view file name is needed when including it. The section then introduces field-level validations, externalized strings, and the UIM pages/views reference.

children.title: 13.4 UIM page field level validations
children.summary: Field-level UIM validations are shown in a cluster above the fields, and their messages may not appear in the same order as the fields themselves. This section is brief and mainly explains the display behavior of validation messages on a page.

children.title: 13.5 Externalized strings
children.summary: UIM documents must externalize all string values and image references, storing the actual values in separate files rather than inline in the UIM. This supports easier maintenance and localization. The section briefly notes this rule and points readers to a more detailed “Externalized strings” reference on page 56.

children.title: 13.6 UIM pages and views reference
Connection types
children.summary: Explains how UIM pages use connections to link page components to data, with connection tags indicating direction: SOURCE, TARGET, or INITIAL. Describes the NAME attribute values for endpoints, including server interface names, TEXT, CONSTANT, JSCRIPT_REF, and PAGE, and when each is allowed. Notes that JSCRIPT_REF is limited to TARGET connections with PAGE or DISPLAY-phase server interfaces as sources, while TEXT/CONSTANT are used with ACTION-phase TARGET server interfaces. Includes an example showing page, field, link, and action-set connections.

children.title: ACTION_CONTROL element
children.summary: Defines the UIM ACTION_CONTROL element, which creates page actions such as links, buttons, file-download links, clipboard actions, dismiss actions, and submit/dismiss combinations. Covers cancel/previous behavior, including limitations of implicit back navigation and recommendations for wizard/tabbed flows. Explains file download configuration via FILE_DOWNLOAD_CONFIG in curam-config.xml and how ACTION_CONTROL or WIDGET can trigger downloads. Lists key attributes like LABEL, TYPE, STYLE, CONFIRM, DEFAULT, ACTION_ID, IMAGE, and ALIGNMENT, plus their rules and constraints.

children.title: ACTION_SET element
children.summary: ACTION_SET groups multiple ACTION_CONTROLs for action menus and other contexts. It supports different display modes: over/under the associated element, left/right/center/default alignment, and LIST_ROW_MENU for list-row action menus. Inline action controls in action menus ignore SEPARATORs, but dropdown menus use SEPARATOR to insert gray dividers. Conditional links hidden by HIDE_CONDITIONAL_LINKS won’t display; otherwise they appear disabled. Key attributes are TOP, BOTTOM, ALIGNMENT, and TYPE.

children.title: CLUSTER element
Dynamic conditional clusters
children.summary: Explains CLUSTER child elements and dynamic conditional clusters. A dynamic cluster uses a CONDITION with TYPE="dynamic" so input fields on the current page can show/hide another cluster at runtime via JavaScript. Supported dynamic controls are dropdowns, radio groups, and single Boolean checkboxes; static data can also come from page or display-phase server interface connections mapped to JSCRIPT_REF. Covers supported data types, submission/reset behavior, nested cluster limits and restrictions, and basic configuration via PAGE SCRIPT_FILE and JavaScript field references.

CLUSTER defines screen layout for related fields, labels, lists, widgets, and nested clusters. It supports key layout attributes such as TITLE, NUM_COLS, TAB_ORDER, SHOW_LABELS, LAYOUT_ORDER, WIDTH, LABEL_WIDTH, BEHAVIOR (collapsible), SUMMARY, and SCROLL_HEIGHT. Child content can mix FIELD, CONTAINER, WIDGET, CLUSTER, LIST, ACTION_SET, and optional TITLE/DESCRIPTION/CONDITION. The section also covers dynamic conditional clusters: using CONDITION TYPE="dynamic" and JavaScript to show/hide clusters based on current-page controls or mapped static data, with limits, supported data types, reset/submission behavior, and configuration guidance.

children.title: CONDITION element
children.summary: Defines the CONDITION element, which controls whether an ACTION_SET, ACTION_CONTROL, LIST, or CLUSTER is displayed. If the condition is true, the parent element is shown; if false, it is hidden, except ACTION_SET/ACTION_CONTROL may still appear as disabled links depending on HIDE_CONDITIONAL_LINKS. The section also illustrates dynamic cluster setup using TYPE="DYNAMIC" on CONDITION, a SCRIPT expression, CONTROL_REF on FIELD elements, and JSCRIPT_REF/page connection values used by JavaScript conditions.

children.title: CONNECT element
children.summary: The CONNECT element defines a data connection between two endpoints, such as server interface bean properties, page parameters, screen controls, or localized string values. It has no attributes. It must include at least one child element, and the meaning of those children depends on the CONNECT element’s context and parent/child definitions. Use this section if you need how UIM data connections are declared and linked.

children.title: CONTAINER element
children.summary: The CONTAINER element groups FIELD, ACTION_CONTROL, and IMAGE elements so they can occupy a single cell in a CLUSTER or LIST. It must contain at least one child element. Key attributes include LABEL and LABEL_ABBREVIATION for display text, WIDTH (default 100) for cell width percentage, ALIGNMENT (LEFT, RIGHT, CENTER, or DEFAULT; default is left aligned), SEPARATOR for text between contained elements, and STYLE for a CSS class.

children.title: DETAILS_ROW element
children.summary: DETAILS_ROW defines expandable list rows in a LIST. Its child content is currently only INLINE_PAGE, shown when a row is expanded. Expanded rows can persist after self-submit or dialog refresh if INLINE_PAGE page parameters uniquely identify each row, but may collapse in several cases (non-matching refreshes, duplicate items, changed parameters, or conditional expandability lost). DETAILS_ROW is not allowed with SCROLL_HEIGHT. The only attribute is MINIMUM_EXPANDED_HEIGHT, default 30px, to increase expanded row height for complex inline pages.

children.title: DESCRIPTION element
children.summary: The DESCRIPTION element defines a description for PAGE_TITLE, CLUSTER, or LIST elements, built by concatenating multiple connection sources. It supports the optional SEPARATOR attribute, which references an externalized string used between parts. Its only child element is CONNECT, required one or more times, and each CONNECT may contain a single SOURCE. SOURCES can be server interface properties or text references when NAME is set to TEXT.

children.title: FIELD element
children.summary: Defines the UIM FIELD element, used to display values in clusters/lists, collect input, or render aggregated cells. Covers dropdown/code-table hierarchy behavior and differences between code table hierarchies and Carbon combo boxes. Documents key rendering rules for edit vs read-only pages and details many attributes: label, description, alt text, width/units, height, alignment, default values, blank option, control modes, config, initial focus, prompt, and control_ref. Useful if you need FIELD configuration or input/display behavior.

children.title: FOOTER_ROW element
children.summary: Defines the FOOTER_ROW element for list footers. A list may have multiple footer rows, and each FOOTER_ROW can contain only FIELD elements, with the FIELD count matching the parent list’s column count. It distinguishes footer field styling: TEXT SOURCE fields use the footerheader CSS class, while all other SOURCE connections use footervalue. Both classes are in curam_common.css and can be customized.

children.title: IMAGE element
children.summary: The IMAGE element inserts an image into a CONTAINER. This section begins the IMAGE element reference and notes that it has attributes, but the detailed attribute list is not shown in the excerpt. Useful if you need how images are embedded in Cúram UIM layouts.

children.title: INCLUDE element
children.summary: The INCLUDE element inserts the contents of an external UIM view document at the current page position. It has one required attribute, FILE_NAME, which must be the file name only—no path. The element has no child elements.

children.title: INITIAL element
children.summary: The INITIAL element is only valid inside a CONNECT element within a FIELD element. It defines the initial connection type used in field-related UI behavior, with examples referenced for pop-up pages and selection lists populated from server interface properties. This section mainly indicates where INITIAL can be used and points to related documentation for its behavior and use cases.

children.title: INFORMATIONAL element
children.summary: The INFORMATIONAL element displays successful server-returned informational messages, not errors. Messages are created in server-side code via the SDEJ Informational Manager API and assigned to output list field(s), referenced through child CONNECT elements. Messages appear at the top of the page in the same area as errors, possibly on a following or parent page for modal dialogs, and are always shown in the main content area, never the context panel. It has no attributes and requires 1..n CONNECT child elements.

children.title: INLINE_PAGE element
children.summary: Defines the INLINE_PAGE element, which embeds one UIM page inside another. It is currently supported only within a LIST’s DETAILS_ROW to show extra content when a row is expanded. Key attributes are PAGE_ID (required), URI_SOURCE_NAME, and URI_SOURCE_PROPERTY. CONNECT child elements can pass page parameters to the inline page. It warns against circular dependencies and notes an inline page cannot also be mapped to a tab. Expanded-row pages are for viewing only; edits should use a modal dialog.

children.title: IS_FALSE element
children.summary: Defines the IS_FALSE boolean test element used in a CONDITION. It evaluates to true when the referenced Boolean property is false. The section lists two required attributes: NAME, the SERVER_INTERFACE instance providing the value, and PROPERTY, the Boolean property name. It also notes there are no child elements and refers to IS_TRUE for related usage details.

children.title: IS_TRUE element
children.summary: Defines the IS_TRUE condition element for UIM pages. It evaluates to true when a referenced Boolean property is true. Required attributes are NAME (the SERVER_INTERFACE instance supplying the property) and PROPERTY (the Boolean property name). It typically reads display-phase server interface properties, but on self-submitting pages with PAGE_ID=THIS it can also reference action-phase properties; before submission that value is treated as false. The element has no child elements.

children.title: JSP SCRIPTLET
children.summary: Defines the JSP_SCRIPTLET UIM element for inserting JSP code at a specific point in a page. Explains that scriptlets can use DISPLAY-phase TextHelper beans via pageContext, and recommends using them mainly for page redirects. Covers XML escaping/CDATA, preserving system parameters via RequestHandler.getSystemParameters() for proper back-navigation, and warns that redirects must be the page’s only child or they may fail. Notes how to pass parameters safely, including escaping non-ASCII values with RequestUtils.escapeURL().

children.title: LABEL element
children.summary: The LABEL element is a child of FIELD used to build a label by concatenating multiple values via CONNECT child elements. It has no attributes. The section includes an example showing a LABEL combining text, display values, and a separator, then passing the field value to an ACTION target. It also begins the next topic, LINK element, but the main focus here is LABEL structure and usage.

children.title: LINK element
Modal dialogs
children.summary: Explains modal dialogs in UIM: they block access to the parent page, differ from pop-up pages, and usually close when navigating unless the same page ID is reopened, DISMISS_MODAL=false keeps the same modal, or OPEN_MODAL=true opens another modal. Shows how to open a link in a modal with OPEN_MODAL="true" and notes OPEN_MODAL is ignored on SUBMIT action controls. Covers WINDOW_OPTIONS (especially width, default 600, unsupported values cause build errors), precedence of PAGE over LINK options, JavaScript APIs for controlling dialogs, custom non-UIM page support, and making modals draggable.

LINK defines navigation targets after actions: internal Cúram pages, external URLs/URIs, home page, and parameter passing via CONNECT. Key attributes include PAGE_ID/PAGE_ID_REF, URL/URI/URI_REF, URI source properties, OPEN_NEW for external sites, and SAVE_LINK for back-navigation behavior. It also covers modal support: OPEN_MODAL opens a modal, DISMISS_MODAL can reuse the same popup, and WINDOW_OPTIONS (width only, default 600) sizes modals; invalid options error. Child CONNECT/CONDITION controls parameters and visibility.

children.title: LIST element
Editable Lists
children.summary: Describes LIST child elements and editable list behavior. Lists must include at least one ACTION_CONTROL, FIELD, or CONTAINER; list properties must share the same server-model structure and length, while non-list values repeat per row. Covers optional TITLE, DESCRIPTION, ACTION_SET, CONDITION, DETAILS_ROW, FOOTER_ROW, LIST_CONNECT, WIDGET, and how fields/containers/actions can mix. Editable lists are FIELD elements with both SOURCE and TARGET, allowing some list items to be user-editable while others remain read-only.

The LIST element defines a tabular control where fields or action controls become columns. It supports title, style, description, sortable headers, summary text, scroll height, collapsible behavior, and pagination settings. A list must contain at least one ACTION_CONTROL, FIELD, or CONTAINER. Child content can include TITLE, DESCRIPTION, ACTION_SET, CONDITION, DETAILS_ROW, FOOTER_ROW, LIST_CONNECT, and WIDGET. List properties must come from the same server-model list structure, with non-list values repeated per row. Editable lists use FIELD elements with both SOURCE and TARGET.

children.title: LIST_CONNECT element
children.summary: Explains the LIST_CONNECT child element of LIST, used on pages with editable lists to pass list data from a source to a target list, including hidden per-row values (for example, IDs). The section also contrasts read-only lists, checkbox-controlled editable lists, and normal editable lists, and notes that only specific data types are supported in editable list fields (boolean, date, numeric, char, string).

children.title: MENU element
DYNAMIC and INTEGRATED_CASE type menus
children.summary: Describes MENU element modes DYNAMIC and INTEGRATED_CASE, which build menus from server-supplied data instead of static action controls. Explains the shared DYNAMIC_MENU XML format with LINK and PARAMETER elements, including PAGE_ID, DESC, and TYPE attributes. Shows how menu icons/text are mapped via DynamicMenuConfig.xml or ICDynamicMenuConfig.xml, and notes that INTEGRATED_CASE differs by using a different root config and no SEPARATOR element.

children.title: The IN_PAGE_NAVIGATION type menu
children.summary: Describes the IN_PAGE_NAVIGATION menu, which renders tab-like links within a UIM page. All pages in the set must define the same MENU, and the active tab is marked with STYLE="in-page-current-link" on the matching ACTION_CONTROL. Also introduces WIZARD_PROGRESS_BAR menus, used to show sequential progress and navigation in modal dialog wizard flows, with wizard pages implemented as standard UIM pages containing the progress bar and next/back controls.

children.title: WIZARD_PROGRESS_BAR menu
children.summary: Explains the WIZARD_PROGRESS_BAR menu for UIM modal dialogs. A menu with MODE="WIZARD_PROGRESS_BAR" binds multiple pages into a sequential wizard, with a progress bar showing step position while users navigate via back/next controls. It should be used only in modal dialogs, not standard pages. Each wizard page is a normal UIM page with the progress bar widget at the top, and action controls should generally keep DISMISS_MODAL="false" except for controls intended to close the modal.

children.title: The UIM wizard pages
children.summary: This section explains UIM in-page navigation tabs and wizard progress bars. It shows how IN_PAGE_NAVIGATION requires the same MENU on each page and marks the current tab with STYLE="in-page-current-link". It also describes WIZARD_PROGRESS_BAR for sequential navigation in modal dialogs, where each wizard page is a standard UIM page with the progress widget at the top and back/forward action controls. Key constraint: wizard progress bars should be used only in modal UIM pages.

children.title: Wizard menu configuration
children.summary: Explains how to configure a wizard menu UIM with a `MENU MODE="WIZARD_PROGRESS_BAR"` and why `SAVE_LINK` should be `false`. Shows that the menu’s `CONNECT` supplies the server-side resource ID for the wizard. Describes the property resource file needed for progress bar text, titles, descriptions, and page IDs, including `Number.Wizard.Pages` and `Wizard.PageID.n`. Notes that `Number.Wizard.Pages` must be a whole number greater than zero.

Defines the MENU element and its modes: STATIC, NAVIGATION, DYNAMIC, INTEGRATED_CASE, IN_PAGE_NAVIGATION, and WIZARD_PROGRESS_BAR. Explains that STATIC/NAVIGATION/IN_PAGE_NAVIGATION use ACTION_CONTROL links, while DYNAMIC and INTEGRATED_CASE are server-driven via a CONNECT to XML data using LINK/PARAMETER entries and icon text mappings from menu config files. Covers in-page tab navigation, wizard progress bars in modal dialogs, required page/link settings, and wizard resource properties such as Number.Wizard.Pages and Wizard.PageID.n.

children.title: PAGE element
children.summary: Defines the UIM PAGE root element used to generate a JSP page. Covers required PAGE_ID and optional attributes for pop-up pages, shared JavaScript (SCRIPT_FILE), label punctuation, modal sizing (WINDOW_OPTIONS), page type (DEFAULT/DETAILS/SPLIT_WINDOW), and conditional-link display. Also lists child elements and their cardinality: INCLUDE, PAGE_TITLE, DESCRIPTION, SHORTCUT_TITLE, SERVER_INTERFACE, INFORMATIONAL, MENU, ACTION_SET, and PAGE_PARAMETER. Useful for understanding page-level UIM structure and configuration.

children.title: PAGE_PARAMETER element
children.summary: Defines the PAGE_PARAMETER element for a page, which declares a parameter available to the current page. Once declared, the parameter can be used as a connection source by setting the SOURCE connection end-point bean NAME to PAGE. The only attribute is required NAME, which specifies the parameter name. The element has no child elements.

children.title: PAGE_TITLE element
children.summary: Defines the PAGE_TITLE element, which controls the title shown at the top of a page’s main content area and, in context panel pages, the tab title. The title can be built from multiple CONNECT sources, including localized strings and server interface data. It supports optional DESCRIPTION, STYLE, and ICON attributes. Child elements include one or more CONNECT elements and an optional DESCRIPTION element; the child DESCRIPTION overrides the DESCRIPTION attribute.

children.title: SCRIPT element
children.summary: Defines the SCRIPT element, which creates an event-triggered JavaScript exit point for pages, read-write fields, and action controls. It is not supported for fields inside a LIST or for read-only fields. Key attributes are EVENT (required; HTML event name), ACTION (required; function call to run), SCRIPT_FILE (optional; script file containing the function, defaulting to the page script file), and EXPRESSION (optional; function identifier used to evaluate dynamic conditional cluster display).

children.title: SERVER_INTERFACE element
children.summary: Defines the SERVER_INTERFACE element, which lets other page elements connect to a server interface. Required attributes are NAME (unique instance name), CLASS (server interface class name), and OPERATION (operation name). Optional PHASE defaults to DISPLAY; DISPLAY calls the interface when the JSP page renders, while ACTION calls it only on ACTION_CONTROL submit events. Only one ACTION-phase SERVER_INTERFACE per PAGE is recommended.

children.title: SOURCE element
children.summary: The SOURCE element defines the source endpoint of a data connection. It can take its value from a server interface property, a page parameter declared with PAGE_PARAMETER, or an externalized string. This section is mainly about where SOURCE values may come from and how it fits into page/data connection configuration.

children.title: TAB_NAME element
children.summary: Defines the TAB_NAME element for UIM context panel pages. It builds the tab bar text by concatenating multiple connection sources, including localized strings and server interface data. Applies only when the PAGE TYPE is DETAILS. Child elements allowed: one or more CONNECT elements, each containing a SOURCE element, and an optional DESCRIPTION element that can override the DESCRIPTION attribute and be assembled from multiple sources.

children.title: TARGET element
children.summary: Defines the TARGET element in Cúram data connections. TARGET specifies the endpoint value to set, either a server interface property or a page parameter to export. It has two required attributes: NAME (server interface name or PAGE for a page parameter) and PROPERTY (the property or output page parameter name). TARGET has no child elements. The excerpt also begins the TITLE element description, which defines titles for CLUSTER/LIST elements via CONNECT sources and optional separator.

children.title: TITLE element
children.summary: The TITLE element defines the title shown at the top of a CLUSTER or LIST element. It builds the title by concatenating multiple connection sources, including localized strings and server interface data.
